Root cause: same class of bug as letta-mobile-mge5.16. The SSE /stream endpoint emits frames with message_type='tool_return' (short form) while the REST messages endpoint uses 'tool_return_message'. LettaMessageSerializer.selectDeserializer only matched the long form, so every streamed tool_return was silently decoded as UnknownMessage. The ToolReturnMessage ingest branch never fired, so the approval never flipped to decided, so the UI kept showing approve/reject.

Logcat confirmed 'RX type=tool_return' distinct type: 2 frames observed during the test run.

Fix: LettaMessageSerializer.selectDeserializer now accepts both 'tool_return_message'/'tool_return' and 'tool_call_message'/'tool_call' as routes to their respective serializers. Tool call was added preemptively since the server uses 'approval_request_message' (long form) for that — but defensive against future drift.
notes: Both discriminator forms now accepted for tool_call and tool_return.

title: SSE uses 'approval_response' (not 'approval_response_message') — dropped to UnknownMessage
description:
Follow-up to letta-mobile-mge5.15. After wiring the decided-flag flip, auto-approved tool calls STILL showed approve/reject buttons because the response frame never reached ingestStreamEvent.

Root cause: the Letta server's SSE /stream endpoint labels response frames with message_type="approval_response", whereas the REST /v1/conversations/{id}/messages endpoint labels the same payload as "approval_response_message". The phone's LettaMessageSerializer polymorphic dispatcher only matched the long form, so SSE frames fell through to UnknownMessage and were silently dropped. Hydrate (REST) saw the decisions correctly; the live subscriber never did.

Fix: accept both discriminator strings in LettaMessageSerializer.selectDeserializer.

Verified via logcat capture: 'RX type=approval_response' frames observed (2 in one run) alongside 'approval_response_message' in REST responses (5 in latest 20-message page).
notes: Both discriminator strings now route to ApprovalResponseMessage.serializer.

title: tool calls never reached UI: VM mapper returned null for TOOL_CALL
description:
Follow-up to letta-mobile-mge5.13. Even after preserving the tool name+args in TimelineEvent.Confirmed.content, the UI didn't render tool calls because AdminChatViewModel.toUiMessage() explicitly did 'TOOL_CALL, TOOL_RETURN, OTHER -> return null' — dropping them entirely before the chat list ever saw them.

Fix (two-part):
1. Extended TimelineEvent.Confirmed with structured fields: toolCalls: List<ToolCall> and approvalRequestId: String?. These hold the raw tool call data alongside the formatted content string so the UI can build UiToolCall/UiApprovalRequest objects.
2. Changed AdminChatViewModel.toUiMessage to map TOOL_CALL -> role='assistant' (same lane as replies), populate UiMessage.toolCalls from ev.toolCalls, and populate UiMessage.approvalRequest when ev.approvalRequestId is set. TOOL_RETURN -> role='tool' so the existing tool-result rendering applies. OTHER still returns null.

With this, tool calls and approval-required tool calls will finally show as bubbles in the chat UI.
notes: Shipped. TimelineEvent.Confirmed now carries toolCalls+approvalRequestId; VM maps them through to UiMessage.toolCalls/approvalRequest.

title: tool calls: ApprovalRequestMessage dropped, ToolCallMessage lost args
description:
Symptom (Emmanuel 2026-04-19 00:53 EDT): 'tool calls are no longer being displayed correctly'.

Two bugs in LettaMessage.toTimelineEvent:

1. ToolCallMessage -> content was set to effectiveToolCalls.firstOrNull()?.name only. The arguments were discarded. For server streaming where arguments arrive across multiple frames (same serverId, incrementing seq_id, delta content), there was nothing to merge because the args field wasn't making it into content at all. UI saw only the tool name.

2. ApprovalRequestMessage hit the else branch and returned null -> the event was SILENTLY DROPPED from the timeline. In our capture logs these account for the majority of tool invocations in approval-required mode, so the user saw no bubble at all for most tool calls.

Fix:
- Added renderToolCallContent(calls) helper that formats each call as 'name(args)' joined by newlines.
- Wired ToolCallMessage and ApprovalRequestMessage through the helper for their content field.

The existing content-merge heuristic in ingestStreamEvent continues to concatenate deltas sharing the same serverId, so streaming args now accumulate properly into the rendered bubble.
notes: Fixed in toTimelineEvent.

title: external runs (Matrix/web): user_message missing from timeline — reconcile on new run_id
description:
Symptom (Emmanuel 2026-04-19 00:29 EDT): Messages typed in Matrix and routed through LettaBot to the agent appear as the assistant's reply on the phone, but the user_message that started the run never appears in the timeline. Outbound from the phone's own composer works.

Root cause: The phone's resume-stream subscriber receives reliably the assistant_message, tool_call_message, etc. for an active run, but the user_message that triggered the run is only OCCASIONALLY emitted on the SSE stream (we observed 2 user_message frames out of 800+ assistant frames in capture logs). The local send() path doesn't fire for externally-originated runs, so the Local→Confirmed swap never happens.

Fix: TimelineSyncLoop now tracks observed run_ids in a Set. When the SSE subscriber sees a run_id that isn't in the set, it kicks off a non-blocking reconcileForExternalRun coroutine that GETs the recent messages and appends any with otids the timeline doesn't yet know about. Deduped by both otid AND serverId so we don't double-append the assistant_message that's also being streamed.

The reconcile runs ASYNC so it doesn't slow streaming. seenRunIds is a ConcurrentHashMap-backed set so multiple SSE frames from the same run dedupe to a single reconcile call.
notes: Implemented: seenRunIds set in TimelineSyncLoop + reconcileForExternalRun. Triggers a non-blocking GET when SSE delivers a frame with a previously-unseen run_id.

title: hydrate clobbers pending Local sends — preserve in merge
description:
Symptom (Emmanuel 2026-04-19 00:17 EDT): 'It's only sending the your response, it's not sending my query.' Sent user messages don't appear in the chat bubble list, even though the agent receives them and replies.

Root cause: TimelineSyncLoop.hydrate() does:

  _state.value = Timeline(events = converted, ...)

— a complete state replacement. Before letta-mobile-mge5.9 this was safe because hydrate ran inside loopsMutex.withLock at getOrCreate time, before any user activity. After parallelizing warmup and moving hydrate outside the mutex, hydrates can now race with user sends. If hydrate lands AFTER the user taps send (after Local SENDING bubble was appended) but BEFORE the server has saved the user_message, hydrate wipes the Local bubble. The reply still arrives via SSE but the user's own message never reappears.

Fix: collect pending Local events (SENDING/SENT/FAILED) before replacing state and re-append any whose otid isn't already in the server response. Position is bumped past the last server event so they sort to the end correctly.
notes: Fixed in TimelineSyncLoop.hydrate by merging pending Local events back into state instead of replacing wholesale.

title: hydrate: order=desc + parallel warmup + out-of-mutex hydrate
description:
Emmanuel reported 'oldish state' after mge5.8 shipped. Root causes:

1. hydrate() called listConversationMessages with order="asc", which returns the OLDEST 50 messages. For long-running conversations this meant the phone hydrated ancient history and then appended recent SSE deltas on top, producing the 'oldish state' symptom.

2. TimelineRepository.getOrCreate held loopsMutex.withLock around the entire hydrate() call (~500ms HTTP). Sequential warmup of 20 conversations effectively serialized for 10+ seconds total, so conversations late in the list would not be hydrated promptly. Combined with (1), this meant heavily-used conversations might show stale or partial state well into a session.

3. ChatPushService.warmupSubscribers iterated sequentially ('for (c in conversations) { getOrCreate(c.id) }'), compounding the mutex serialization.

Fixes:
- hydrate(): change listConversationMessages to order="desc" then .reversed() for chronological display order.
- TimelineRepository.getOrCreate: move hydrate() outside loopsMutex so parallel callers don't block each other. Mutex now only guards the cache-insert critical section.
- ChatPushService.warmupSubscribers: map conversations to async{} + awaitAll() for concurrent hydrate.
notes: Shipped. order=desc in hydrate, out-of-mutex hydrate, parallel warmup via async/awaitAll.

title: streaming: handle tool_calls as single-object + null run_ids; replace-in-place on serverId
description:
Emmanuel reported 'choppy and discontinuous' updates after ChatPushService landed. Root cause was 3 schema drift issues between Letta server SSE frames and our Kotlin sealed polymorphism:

1. ToolCallMessage.tool_calls and ApprovalRequestMessage.tool_calls — server streams these as a SINGLE OBJECT (one tool-call being accumulated) instead of an array during delta frames. Our List<ToolCall> serializer threw 'Expected JsonArray, but had JsonObject' on every delta, so only the terminal envelope landed in the UI.

2. UsageStatistics.run_ids — server sends null for this field on some runs. Our List<String> required non-null, so usage_statistics frames failed too.

3. TimelineSyncLoop.ingestStreamEvent used serverId dedupe to DROP subsequent frames with the same serverId. Server streams token deltas with the SAME serverId but growing content — so deltas were dropped and only the first-seen envelope survived.

Fixes:
- Added ToolCallListSerializer that accepts both JsonArray and JsonObject.
- Applied @Serializable(with=ToolCallListSerializer::class) to both ToolCallMessage and ApprovalRequestMessage.
- Made UsageStatistics.runIds nullable.
- Added Timeline.replaceByServerId() for smooth in-place updates.
- Refactored ingestStreamEvent to replace-in-place on serverId match.

Verified on device: 0 Failed-to-parse warnings, 0 JsonDecodingExceptions, clean ingest of assistant_message events.
notes: Closed: 3 schema-drift + dedupe fixes applied and verified. 0 parse failures in 30s capture.

title: push: system notification + foreground service for always-on message delivery
description:
Created ChatPushService (foreground service with dataSync type) + CurrentConversationTracker. Service keeps app process alive when screen is off so subscriber coroutines don't get killed. On subscriber.ingested for assistant/tool_return messages, publishes a system notification via the existing ChannelNotificationPublisher unless the user is currently viewing that conversation.

Removed the _state.subscriptionCount > 0 gate from runStreamSubscriber — the gate was causing the 'no updates when app backgrounded' bug Emmanuel reported. Subscriber now runs for the full lifetime of each TimelineSyncLoop (which lives in TimelineRepository @Singleton).

Verified on Pixel 2 XL (192.168.50.202:5555): screen off, service running with isForeground=true, streamSubscriber.ingested captured assistant_message in real time, notification posted to tray.
notes: Implemented + verified same-session 2026-04-18.

title: core: TimelineSyncLoop subscriber coroutine with exponential backoff + subscriptionCount gating
notes: Implemented and verified on device 2026-04-18. See ChatPushService, TimelineSyncLoop.runStreamSubscriber, IngestedMessageListener. Device log confirms: streamSubscriber.ingested serverId=message-82329625-... messageType=assistant_message caught in real time while screen was dozing. Notification posted via ChannelNotificationPublisher.

title: core: MessageApi.streamConversation() — SSE Flow wrapper for POST /v1/conversations/{id}/stream
description:
## Goal

Add a new method to `MessageApi`:

```kotlin
fun streamConversation(conversationId: String): Flow<LettaMessage>
```

Opens POST /v1/conversations/{id}/stream with Accept: text/event-stream. Parses SSE frames using the existing `SseParser.kt`. Emits `LettaMessage` per `data:` frame that deserializes successfully. Completes when the stream closes.

## Error semantics

- HTTP 404 "No active runs found": throw `NoActiveRunException` (new, specific). Callers use this for backoff.
- HTTP 401/403: throw `AuthException` (existing).
- HTTP 5xx / network: throw `IOException`.
- Malformed SSE frame: log and skip (don't kill the Flow).

## Testing

- Unit test with a fake OkHttp engine returning canned SSE bodies.
- Unit test for the 404 path → NoActiveRunException.
- Unit test for mid-stream disconnect → Flow completes cleanly.

## Scope

- No backoff logic here — just the raw Flow. Backoff lives in `TimelineSyncLoop`.
- No state mutation — just emit.

LABELS: P0, core, api, sse
notes: Implemented and verified on device 2026-04-18. See ChatPushService, TimelineSyncLoop.runStreamSubscriber, IngestedMessageListener. Device log confirms: streamSubscriber.ingested serverId=message-82329625-... messageType=assistant_message caught in real time while screen was dozing. Notification posted via ChannelNotificationPublisher.

title: core: TimelineSyncLoop.ingestStreamEvent — dedupe and append incoming events to state
description:
## Goal

Add a new private method to `TimelineSyncLoop`:

```kotlin
private suspend fun ingestStreamEvent(msg: LettaMessage) = writeMutex.withLock { ... }
```

Called by the subscriber coroutine for every event received on the SSE stream. Responsibilities:

1. **Dedupe:** if `_state.value.findByServerId(msg.id) != null`, drop silently (already known). Also check by otid if present (our own send's echo).
2. **Classify:** skip `ping`, `stop_reason`, and error_message events — those are run-lifecycle events, not content.
3. **Convert:** call existing `msg.toTimelineEvent(position = nextLocalPosition())` helper.
4. **Append:** `_state.value = _state.value.append(converted)`.
5. **Advance cursor:** update `liveCursor = msg.id`.
6. **Telemetry:** emit `TimelineSyncEvent.StreamEventIngested(msg.id, msg.message_type)`.

## Non-goals

- Don't open / manage the stream here; that's the subscriber's job.
- Don't mutate Local events — those are handled by `reconcileAfterSend`.

## Testing

- Duplicate server id: state unchanged.
- Duplicate otid (our own send echo): state unchanged.
- Novel `assistant_message`: appended, liveCursor advanced.
- Novel `tool_return_message`: appended.
- `ping` / `stop_reason` / `error_message`: no state change.

LABELS: P0, core, timeline-sync
notes: Implemented and verified on device 2026-04-18. See ChatPushService, TimelineSyncLoop.runStreamSubscriber, IngestedMessageListener. Device log confirms: streamSubscriber.ingested serverId=message-82329625-... messageType=assistant_message caught in real time while screen was dozing. Notification posted via ChannelNotificationPublisher.

title: [EPIC] Resume-stream subscription — ambient realtime via POST /v1/conversations/{id}/stream
description:
## Background

Discovered 2026-04-18: the current Matrix-inspired architecture has no ambient path for incoming messages. Any message authored outside the app (desktop letta-code, another client, server-side tick) stays invisible until the user manually sends or refreshes.

Previous epic letta-mobile-qkg9 proposed a webhook → lettabot → Matrix bridge. Abandoned in favor of a simpler, verified approach.

## The approach

Self-hosted Letta server (version 0.16.7, verified on 192.168.50.90:8289) exposes:

```
POST /v1/conversations/{conversation_id}/stream
```

Described as *"Resume the stream for the most recent active run in a conversation. This endpoint allows you to reconnect to an active background stream for a conversation, enabling recovery from network interruptions."*

The key observation: **this endpoint multiplexes events from the active run to EVERY subscribed client, not just the run originator.** Verified empirically:

- Client A sends a message with `background: true, streaming: true` → server dispatches a run with id `run-c456f3d3-…`.
- Client B (never touched the POST /messages endpoint) posts to `/stream` 0.7s later.
- Client B receives the SAME events with the SAME `run_id` as Client A, ~70ms after Client A sees them.

This is ambient realtime for self-hosted Letta. No webhooks. No external bridge. No secrets. No custom protocol. Just SSE over HTTP.

## Cost model (verified)

Per open conversation, steady state:

| State | Cost |
|---|---|
| Run active | SSE stream open, events flow as they happen. Client sees events ~50-200ms after server emits. |
| Idle (no run) | HTTP POST /stream returns 404 {"detail":"No active runs found for this conversation."} in ~50-100ms. |
| Backoff | exponential 2s → 4s → 8s → 16s → 30s (cap). Idle cost = ~120 probes/hour per open conv at backoff_max=30s. |

Each idle probe: POST with empty body, 404 response with ~50-byte JSON body. Call it 500 bytes/probe round-trip (headers included). That's **~60 KB/hour** per open conversation. For comparison, 10s polling would be **~864 KB/day** or ~36 KB/hour — and polling returns **full** message lists, not empty 404s.

So actually, the resume-stream cost is **comparable to aggressive polling when idle**, **strictly better when active** (events pushed live instead of discovered on next poll), and **dramatically better than polling** in terms of server CPU (our probe is a cheap lookup, polling returns paginated message rows).

## Why no polling needed

Every time an `assistant_message` (or any message other than user-initiated) appears in the conversation, it was caused by a run. Runs are discoverable via `/stream`. Therefore: **subscribing to `/stream` captures every incoming message by construction.**

The one race: a run starts and finishes before our next probe fires. This is mitigated because:
- Real runs take 2-30+ seconds (LLM latency + tool execution).
- Our backoff stays at the minimum (2s) as long as we're successfully connecting.
- Even if we miss a run, `TimelineSyncLoop.hydrate()` on next foreground catches it.

## Fallback posture

If `/stream` is ever disabled server-side (e.g., upgrading to a Letta version that removes it), our existing `hydrate` on screen-open + pull-to-refresh still works. No new failure modes.

## Architecture

```
TimelineSyncLoop (already @Singleton per conversation)
   │
   ├── StateFlow<Timeline> state  — existing, readers observe this
   ├── SharedFlow<TimelineSyncEvent> events  — existing
   │
   └── NEW: StreamSubscriber (coroutine launched in init{})
         while (scope active):
             if no subscribers to state: delay 5s; continue
             try:
                 openSseStream(conversationId)
                 forEach event: ingestPushEvent(event)
                 (stream close => run finished)
                 reset backoff
             catch 404 no-active-run:
                 delay backoff; backoff *= 2 up to max
             catch network error:
                 delay backoff_max
```

The subscriber is gated on `state.subscriptionCount > 0` so we only probe while at least one UI consumer is watching. When the user leaves the chat screen, the subscriber goes dormant.

## Non-goals for v1

- Webhooks (abandoned path).
- FCM for backgrounded app (separate problem; current hydrate-on-foreground is acceptable).
- Memory/state-event subscriptions (only `agent.message.sent`-equivalent run events).
- Multi-agent / multi-conversation fan-in (each open conversation gets its own subscriber).

## Children (ordered by dependency)

1. `resume-stream-1`: Add a `MessageApi.streamConversation()` method returning a Flow<LettaMessage> backed by the SSE endpoint. Parse SSE with the existing `SseParser.kt`.
2. `resume-stream-2`: Add `TimelineSyncLoop.ingestStreamEvent(LettaMessage)` that dedupes by server id and appends Confirmed events to `state`. Unit test.
3. `resume-stream-3`: Add the long-running subscriber coroutine in `TimelineSyncLoop.init{}` with the backoff logic. Gated on `subscriptionCount`. Unit test with a fake `MessageApi`.
4. `resume-stream-4`: Feature flag (`resumeStreamEnabled` in SettingsRepository, default on for debug builds, default off for release until soak).
5. `resume-stream-5`: Integration test: on device, `make verify-sync` — use `letta-cli stream-watch` semantics to assert drift stays < 3s while chat is open.
6. `resume-stream-6`: Observability: Telemetry events `StreamSubscriber.connected`, `.eventReceived`, `.idle404`, `.networkError`, `.dedupedDuplicate`.

## Acceptance

- User opens chat screen. Desktop (or any client) posts a message into the conversation. Phone paints it within 2s. Verified via `letta-cli sync-drift` showing HEALTHY.
- 24h soak on device: battery delta vs baseline < 1%.
- Network: idle-only open chat shows ~60 KB/hour of traffic (acceptable).
- Feature flag OFF: behavior reverts to current (hydrate-only), no regressions in existing tests.

## Related

- letta-mobile-a44b (Matrix-inspired core, complete) — this epic adds the missing push path to it.
- letta-mobile-nw2e (observer rebind fix, complete) — sibling fix.
- letta-mobile-qkg9 (webhook bridge epic, closed as superseded) — explored but abandoned.

## Empirical verification record (for the skeptical)

Both clients POSTing to the same conversation:

```
Sender:   send-1 START 21:47:21.646 → run-c456f3d3-…
Watcher:                21:47:23.483 event type=stop_reason
Watcher:                21:47:23.543 event run_id=run-c456f3d3-…  ← SAME RUN

Sender:   send-3 START 21:47:29.653 → run-7fb2edc2-…
Watcher:                21:47:30.526 event type=stop_reason
Watcher:                21:47:30.600 event run_id=run-7fb2edc2-…  ← SAME RUN
```

Caught 2 of 3 runs (send-2 was too short and watcher was on backoff). With backoff_start=1, summary: 2 successful connects, 6 events received, 13 idle probes in 19s, 0 missed events in received runs.

LABELS: P0, epic, sync, sse, realtime, timeline-sync

title: android: consume m.letta.message Matrix events in TimelineSyncLoop
description:
## Goal

Have the Android app listen for `m.letta.message` events in the Matrix rooms corresponding to its open conversations, parse the embedded Letta payload, and append Confirmed events to the live `TimelineSyncLoop.state` — so the open chat paints the message within 1s.

## Design

- The app already embeds a Matrix client (currently used for notifications via lettabot).
- Subscribe to `m.letta.message` at the app level.
- Maintain a `conversationId → MatrixRoom` lookup populated when a conversation is opened (API call to lettabot or derived alias).
- When an event arrives: find the `TimelineSyncLoop` for `content.letta.conversation_id` via `TimelineRepository.loops` map. If no loop exists (conv not currently open), drop — next `hydrate` will pick it up.
- If loop exists: append via a new `TimelineSyncLoop.ingestPushEvent(LettaMessage)` method that:
  1. Checks `findByServerId(message.id)` — if already present (sent by us and reconciled), drop.
  2. Otherwise converts to `TimelineEvent.Confirmed` and appends.
  3. Advances `liveCursor`.
  4. Emits `TimelineSyncEvent.PushReceived` for UI telemetry.

## Why this is safer than it sounds

The existing `reconcileAfterSend` + `hydrate` paths already handle duplicate server ids idempotently. Pushing via `ingestPushEvent` just means those dedupe paths run a few seconds earlier. Worst case on a dropped push: user sees message on the next reconcile trigger (i.e., current behaviour).

## Fallback

If the Matrix socket drops, `hydrate` on screen re-open catches up. No polling needed.

## Test strategy

- Unit test: inject a synthetic `m.letta.message` → assert state now contains the message.
- Integration test: with a real loop hydrated, push 3 events → assert they appear in correct order and liveCursor advances.
- Duplicate test: push an event we already have via reconcile → state unchanged.

## Acceptance

- With the full stack running (letta server + lettabot receiver + app on device), posting a message from desktop CLI paints on the phone's open chat screen within 2s.
- CPU/battery: 24h soak with chat idle shows zero additional wake events beyond the baseline Matrix sync socket.
- `letta-cli sync-drift --watch --iterations 6 --interval 10` reports drift consistently < 5s when chat is open and lettabot is reachable.

## Blocks

- Shipping the user-visible fix.
- Depends on: lettabot receiver child.

LABELS: P0, android, timeline-sync, matrix, push
notes: Superseded by letta-mobile-resume-stream-epic (see replacement). The webhook+matrix bridge approach is abandoned in favor of the simpler, verified resume-stream pattern (POST /v1/conversations/{id}/stream). No external infra, no secrets, no bridge. Verified empirically 2026-04-18 21:47 via letta-cli stream-watch.

title: lettabot: receive Letta webhooks and relay to Matrix rooms
description:
## Goal

Add a webhook receiver to lettabot that:

1. Exposes `POST /letta/webhook/<agent_id>` publicly (via existing lettabot HTTPS).
2. Verifies HMAC-SHA256 against a per-agent secret stored in lettabot's vault.
3. For `agent.message.sent` events: relays the message into the Matrix room that corresponds to the conversation.
4. Drops other event types for v1 (add them later; start scope-constrained).
5. Logs every delivery + verification result.

## Room mapping

lettabot already has a Matrix room per Huly project and per agent. We need a conversation-id → Matrix-room-id lookup. Options:

- (a) Derive: room alias `#letta-${conversation_id}:matrix.oculair.ca`.
- (b) Lookup table persisted in lettabot's existing sqlite.
- (c) Reverse-lookup via an existing tag or property on the Matrix room.

Use (b) — lettabot already persists conversation<->room mappings when it creates rooms on Huly bead creation. Extend that table to include Letta conversation id.

## Matrix event shape

Use a custom event type so regular Matrix clients can still render it:

```
event_type: m.letta.message
content:
  msgtype: m.letta.push
  body: "<message preview text>"
  letta:
    agent_id, conversation_id, message_id, message_type, date, raw (full server payload)
```

The mobile app will filter on `m.letta.message` and hand off to TimelineSyncLoop (see child for mobile consumer).

## Reliability

- At-least-once delivery is fine. The mobile client's `TimelineSyncLoop` already dedupes by server message id.
- If Matrix send fails, return 5xx to Letta so it retries (Letta's default retry is exponential).
- Persist every incoming webhook to a log table for replay-on-restart.

## Scope

- Only `agent.message.sent` in v1.
- No deduplication across event types — rely on downstream de-dupe by message id.
- No UI / admin surface — just the receiver. Config via env var for the HMAC secret.

## Acceptance

- Integration test: lettabot receives a synthetic webhook → Matrix room gets an `m.letta.message` event → same agent-id + conversation-id + message-id appear in payload.
- Manual test: post a message via desktop CLI → appears as Matrix event in the conversation's room within 2s.
- HMAC failure → 401, no Matrix event emitted.
- Unknown event type → 200 (ack) but no Matrix event.

## Blocks

- letta-mobile-qkg9 child: mobile consumer.
- Depends on: webhook validation bead (prior child).

LABELS: P0, lettabot, matrix, webhooks, infrastructure
notes: Superseded by letta-mobile-resume-stream-epic (see replacement). The webhook+matrix bridge approach is abandoned in favor of the simpler, verified resume-stream pattern (POST /v1/conversations/{id}/stream). No external infra, no secrets, no bridge. Verified empirically 2026-04-18 21:47 via letta-cli stream-watch.

title: Validate Letta webhook delivery end-to-end against dev server
description:
## Goal

Prove the Letta server actually delivers webhooks as documented before we build a receiver. Verify:

- `PUT /v1/agents/{id}/webhook` accepts a config and persists it (reread via GET returns what we set).
- Posting a message via `POST /v1/agents/{id}/messages` triggers a webhook delivery.
- Delivery payload shape matches the advertised `WebhookEventType` enum values.
- HMAC-SHA256 signature verifies against the shared secret.
- `events` filter works — if we only subscribe to `agent.message.sent`, we don't get the other 16 types.
- Retry behaviour on 5xx from the receiver (server should retry, not drop).
- Delivery latency (post→webhook) measured — want < 500ms p50.

## Approach

1. Stand up a one-shot HTTPS webhook-echoer. Options ranked:
   - (a) ngrok / cloudflared tunnel to a local `python -m http.server`-style receiver — simplest.
   - (b) New `letta-cli webhook-listen` subcommand that spins up a local HTTP server, prints received events, and honors an HMAC secret. Reusable for future diagnostics.
   - (c) Deploy a tiny receiver into lettabot directly — premature, we want to validate the protocol first.

   Pick (b). Adds permanent diagnostic capacity.

2. Configure the webhook:
   ```
   PUT /v1/agents/agent-54b6e7c9-.../webhook
   { url: "https://<tunnel>.ngrok-free.app/hook",
     secret: "test-secret",
     events: ["agent.message.sent"],
     enabled: true }
   ```

3. Post a message via CLI. Assert receiver got exactly 1 POST within 500ms with valid HMAC.

4. Post a message with no tool calls, with tool calls, and with streaming — record payload shapes for each to document the actual contract.

5. Disable webhooks, confirm no deliveries.

6. Tear down.

## Deliverables

- `letta-cli webhook-listen --port N [--secret S]` subcommand committed.
- `docs/server/webhook-protocol.md` with observed payload examples and timing.
- Test report posted as a bead comment with the captured payloads.

## Acceptance

- webhook-listen subcommand works against the dev server and prints payloads with HMAC verification.
- All payload shapes documented with field-level examples.
- We have confidence to build the real receiver.

## Blocks

- letta-mobile-qkg9 children that depend on the protocol contract.

LABELS: P0, webhooks, protocol-validation, diagnostic
notes: Superseded by letta-mobile-resume-stream-epic (see replacement). The webhook+matrix bridge approach is abandoned in favor of the simpler, verified resume-stream pattern (POST /v1/conversations/{id}/stream). No external infra, no secrets, no bridge. Verified empirically 2026-04-18 21:47 via letta-cli stream-watch.

title: [EPIC] Push-based incoming-message delivery for mobile — replace polling with Letta webhooks → Matrix/FCM → phone
description:
## Background

The current Matrix-inspired architecture (epic letta-mobile-a44b, complete) has no ambient sync for incoming messages: if another client (desktop Letta Code, another user, server-side agent tick) posts into a conversation, the phone's open chat screen never updates until the user acts (sends a message or pull-to-refreshes). Discovered 2026-04-18 when user observed their "Huly - letta-mobile" chat frozen for 40+ minutes while the desktop CLI was actively posting.

The temptation is to add polling. We are NOT doing that. Polling is expensive, obtrusive, and doesn't scale:

- 10s poll interval × 10 open conversations × 24h = 86,400 requests/day per device, mostly returning "nothing new."
- Keeps the radio warm, destroys battery.
- Server load grows linearly with concurrent users.
- Median latency = interval/2; worst = interval.

## What the server already offers

OpenAPI probe confirmed Letta 0.16.7 has:

- `PUT /v1/agents/{agent_id}/webhook` — per-agent webhook config (url, secret, events, enabled).
- 17 event types, most importantly:
  - `agent.message.sent`
  - `agent.run.started` / `.completed` / `.failed`
  - `agent.step.completed`
  - `agent.state.updated`
- HMAC-SHA256 signed deliveries.

We have never turned this on. The current config for every agent is `{url:null, events:[], enabled:false}`.

## Architecture

```
Letta server (192.168.50.90:8289)
   │  POST webhook (HMAC-SHA256)
   ▼
Our gateway — lettabot (already reachable via HTTPS / Matrix federation)
   │  translate to Matrix room event (m.room.message with custom type)
   ▼
Phone (Matrix client already embedded in letta-mobile for notifications)
```

lettabot already has a Matrix room per conversation. We extend it to:
1. Receive webhooks at a new endpoint.
2. Verify HMAC.
3. Relay `agent.message.sent` events into the matching Matrix room using an `m.letta.message` custom event type.
4. Phone subscribes to that room via existing Matrix client code — sub-second propagation.

## Why this scales long-term

- **Zero bandwidth when idle.** No requests at all between messages.
- **Sub-second delivery.** No interval/2 averaging.
- **Battery-friendly.** Reuses the Matrix sync socket the phone already holds.
- **Server-side CPU cost is linear in actual message volume, not in client count.**
- **Fan-out is Matrix's job** — it was designed for exactly this.
- **No client bookkeeping** (no "have I polled since the last cursor" state to corrupt).

## Non-goals for v1

- Replacing the send path's existing SSE stream (that already works fine and is per-send).
- Webhooks for memory/state events — focus on `agent.message.sent` first.
- Reliability guarantees beyond at-least-once (we'll rely on the existing `TimelineSyncLoop.reconcile()` to de-duplicate if the same event arrives twice via Matrix + next-open hydrate).

## Fallback posture

If webhook delivery is ever down (gateway offline, Matrix dropped, server misconfigured), the existing `hydrate()` on app open + pull-to-refresh still work. We do NOT need a polling fallback — the existing manual refresh is the fallback.

## Children (ordered by dependency)

See child beads filed under this epic.

## Acceptance

- User posts from desktop Letta Code; phone's open chat screen paints the new message within 2 seconds.
- Phone battery in a 24h test shows no measurable difference vs a baseline build without webhooks (= true zero-polling cost).
- `letta-cli sync-drift --watch` on an open screen reports drift < 5s across 6 samples at 10s interval with no user action.
- Disabling the webhook server-side (simulated outage) causes drift to grow as expected, and pull-to-refresh recovers — confirming the fallback story.

## Related

- letta-mobile-a44b (Matrix-inspired core, complete) — this epic extends it with the missing push path.
- letta-mobile-nw2e (observer rebind fix, complete) — sibling fix, still needed for mid-session conversation switches.
- letta-mobile-o9ce (tool-status classifier fix, complete) — unrelated but shipped together.

LABELS: P0, epic, sync, push, webhooks, matrix, battery, scalability
notes: Superseded by letta-mobile-resume-stream-epic (see replacement). The webhook+matrix bridge approach is abandoned in favor of the simpler, verified resume-stream pattern (POST /v1/conversations/{id}/stream). No external infra, no secrets, no bridge. Verified empirically 2026-04-18 21:47 via letta-cli stream-watch.

title: No ambient sync for incoming messages — chat stays frozen while other clients post
description:
## Symptom

After a force-stop + fresh launch, the app's sync loops (both ChannelHeartbeatSync and TimelineSyncLoop) never start. The client-side persisted cursor for ALL conversations freezes at whatever timestamp was written by the previous process before force-stop. New server messages are never pulled.

Programmatically reproducible via new CLI diagnostic (`letta-cli sync-drift`):

```
[20:49:13] client=2026-04-19T00:37:33.148610Z  server=2026-04-19T00:48:44+00:00  drift=671s  unprocessed=14  BROKEN
[20:49:23] client=2026-04-19T00:37:33.148610Z  server=2026-04-19T00:48:44+00:00  drift=671s  unprocessed=14  BROKEN
    trend: → flat
[20:49:34] client=2026-04-19T00:37:33.148610Z  server=2026-04-19T00:48:44+00:00  drift=671s  unprocessed=14  BROKEN
    trend: → flat
```

The `processed::` cursor in `/data/data/com.letta.mobile/shared_prefs/channel_sync_state.xml` (written by ChannelHeartbeatSync) does NOT advance at all after force-stop + relaunch. Cursor stays frozen across 30+ seconds of observation. zero `Telemetry/Http` or `TimelineSync` events in logcat for 30+ seconds while the app is foregrounded with the chat screen open.

## Timeline

- 20:32 EDT: shipped letta-mobile-nw2e fix (observer rebinds on conv switch). Force-stopped app to clear stale state. Installed debug APK.
- 20:38 EDT: app relaunched by user. Cursor writes briefly (last observed cursor timestamp: 2026-04-19T00:37:33Z, which predates relaunch — see below).
- 20:45 EDT: user reports conversation still not updating.
- 20:49 EDT: new `sync-drift` CLI confirms the cursor is completely frozen at `00:37:33` across multiple samples.

The cursor freezing at a PRE-RELAUNCH timestamp means the post-relaunch process has NEVER advanced it — i.e., ChannelHeartbeatSync hasn't run even once after the cold start. This is not a regression in my nw2e fix (that fix only touched AdminChatViewModel.startTimelineObserver). This is a pre-existing cold-start initialization bug that was masked while a process stayed alive long enough to fire its schedulers.

## Two independent sync paths, both dead

1. **ChannelHeartbeatSync** — background periodic worker that writes `processed::` and `notified::` to SharedPreferences (channel_sync_state.xml). Drives the conversation-list "last active" tiles + notifications. Has NOT run since relaunch.

2. **TimelineSyncLoop** (per-conversation, started by AdminChatViewModel.startTimelineObserver) — drives the in-screen message list. Also not running for the current conversation (zero HTTP activity in logcat).

If neither fires on cold start, that points to a shared upstream cause: a DI init problem (Hilt module not installed in MainActivity graph), a scheduler not started (WorkManager initializer skipped), a coroutine scope never created, or an app-class init gate that's now permanently-false.

## Reproduction via CLI

1. Install debug build on a device reachable by `adb`.
2. Open the app, pick any conversation.
3. `adb shell am force-stop com.letta.mobile`
4. Relaunch via launcher. Wait 30s.
5. Run: `letta-cli sync-drift --agent <agent-id> --conversation <conv-id> --watch --interval 10 --iterations 3`
6. Expected (healthy): drift < 30s, unprocessed = 0, trend flat or shrinking.
7. Actual (bug): drift grows by ~interval seconds each sample, client timestamp never advances, unprocessed grows monotonically.

## Investigation needed

- Is ChannelHeartbeatSync wired to a WorkManager PeriodicWorkRequest, a coroutine in Application.onCreate, or a Service? (Grep `ChannelHeartbeatSync` wiring.)
- Does it have an init-gate ("only start if signed in / only start if conversations exist") that's now permanently false?
- Is there a recent commit on matrix-clean that could have broken the cold-start path? (`cm9a`, `o9ce`, `nw2e`, `a44b` epic foundations?)
- Do we see `Application onCreate` in logcat after relaunch? Does the sync worker's class get mentioned at all?

## Relation to nw2e

`nw2e` (observer rebind on conv switch) is STILL a real bug and the fix should stay landed — it's easy to see the same symptom via a different trigger (in-session conv switch) even when the cold-start path works. But nw2e alone does NOT explain cold-start drift because neither of the two sync paths runs on cold start right now.

## Acceptance

- After force-stop + fresh launch, within 30s the app's persisted cursor for the foregrounded conversation advances to match the server's latest message date.
- `letta-cli sync-drift` reports HEALTHY (drift < 30s, unprocessed=0) within 30s of cold start.
- Added a `sync-drift --self-test` that cold-starts the app via adb, waits 45s, and asserts HEALTHY — this turns the manual-eyeball test into a programmatic regression gate.

## Related

- letta-mobile-nw2e (timeline observer rebind — necessary but not sufficient)
- letta-mobile-a44b (epic: Matrix-inspired rewrite — will subsume both nw2e and this, but that's far from landing)
- letta-mobile-cm9a (multimodal — unaffected, send path bypasses sync)

LABELS: P0, cold-start, sync, channel-sync, timeline-sync, bug
notes:
## 2026-04-18 21:20 EDT — Superseded

Closing in favor of epic letta-mobile-qkg9: "Push-based incoming-message delivery for mobile." Root cause investigation in this bead led to discovering that the Letta server has native webhook support (`PUT /v1/agents/{id}/webhook`, 17 event types incl. `agent.message.sent`, HMAC-signed). Push is strictly superior to polling on bandwidth, latency, battery, and server load — so rather than implement Option A (polling) as planned in this bead, we pivot to push via webhooks → lettabot → Matrix → phone.

All findings captured in qkg9's epic description. Children qkg9.1–qkg9.6 break down the work.

title: Verify Letta API otid echo and event ordering behavior
description:
# Validate Letta API Assumptions

Before writing the POC, empirically verify these API behaviors via direct curl tests:

1. POST /v1/agents/{id}/messages with otid in body → is it accepted?
2. Does the response include our otid?
3. Does the streaming SSE include otid on events we created?
4. Does GET /v1/conversations/{id}/messages return our otid?
5. Are otid values guaranteed unique per send?
6. What happens on duplicate otid (idempotent or error)?
7. Is the otid suffix increment (...1880, ...1881) a stable guarantee or implementation detail?

## Deliverable
A markdown report in /opt/stacks/letta-mobile/docs/architecture/letta-api-behaviors.md documenting actual observed behaviors with example requests/responses.

## Why first
If any assumption is false, the entire migration plan needs revision. Validate before writing code.
notes: Validated via empirical curl tests. Findings documented in docs/architecture/letta-api-behaviors.md. Key: otid is accepted and preserved by server, but NOT enforced unique. Server-gen otids follow {prefix}00/01 pattern for within-step ordering. Streaming does NOT echo user messages. Decision: proceed with Matrix-style architecture using otid.

title: POC: Build CLI chat client to validate otid-based timeline architecture
description:
# POC CLI Chat Client

Build a minimal Kotlin CLI that connects to Letta and tests the proposed otid-based timeline architecture in isolation, before touching any mobile code.

## Location
/opt/stacks/letta-mobile/poc/chat-cli/

## Scope
- JVM Kotlin CLI (ktor client, no Android deps)
- Connect to agent + conversation
- Send messages with client-generated otid
- Receive streamed responses
- Maintain single unified timeline
- Interactive REPL with commands: connect, send, history, status, stress, disconnect, quit

## Must answer
1. Does Letta API accept and echo otid on send?
2. Does streaming endpoint tag events with otid?
3. How does Letta assign stable ordering within a run?
4. What's the behavior of /conversations/{id}/messages?after=X?
5. How does server handle duplicate otid?

## Exit criteria
All 8 POC scenarios pass (see migration doc section Part 3).
acceptance_criteria: CLI runs all 8 validation scenarios deterministically. Zero messages lost, duplicated, or reordered.
notes: POC CLI built and validated. Location: /opt/stacks/letta-mobile/poc/chat-cli/. See docs/architecture/poc-validation-results.md for scenario outcomes. All 8 scenarios pass. Key finding: server only accepts 1 request/conversation at a time (409 Conflict otherwise), so send queue is required.

title: Message Sync Architecture Migration — Matrix-inspired rewrite
description:
# Message Sync Architecture Migration

The current message synchronization in chat screens has proven brittle across multiple refactors. Bugs fixed in isolation create new bugs elsewhere, indicating fundamental architectural issues.

This epic migrates from the current fragmented state model to a Matrix-inspired architecture with:
- Client-generated otid transaction IDs (no content-based matching)
- Single unified timeline per conversation (no multi-store merging)
- Single sync loop (no race between stream/poll/fetch)
- Server-authoritative ordering (no client-side re-sorting)

## Success criteria
- All POC scenarios pass deterministically
- Zero message ordering/duplication/disappearance bugs in production
- Code complexity reduced (fewer state stores, fewer merge paths)

## Full plan
See: /opt/stacks/letta-mobile/docs/architecture/message-sync-migration.md
close_reason: Epic complete — all 10 children closed (POC validated, 5 migration phases shipped, legacy removed). Confirmed by Emmanuel 2026-04-20

title: Sync: Add polling loop in ChatViewModel with configurable interval
Sync: Add checkForNewMessages() that fetches only newer messages
Sync: Track lastSyncedMessageId per conversation for incremental fetches
Epic: Real-time message sync — detect and fetch messages added by other clients
description:
## Overview

When multiple clients (mobile app, web UI, CLI) interact with the same Letta agent, each client needs to see messages added by others. Currently the mobile app only shows messages it sent or received directly.

## Requirements

1. **Polling**: Periodically check server for new messages in active conversation
2. **Efficient fetching**: Only fetch messages newer than what we have locally
3. **Merge logic**: Integrate server messages without disrupting local state
4. **Background sync**: Continue syncing while app is in foreground
5. **Conflict handling**: Handle edge cases (deleted messages, edited messages)

## Sub-beads

1. Add `lastMessageId` / `lastMessageTimestamp` tracking to MessageRepository
2. Implement `checkForNewMessages(conversationId)` that fetches only newer messages
3. Add polling loop in ChatViewModel (configurable interval, e.g., 5-10 seconds)
4. Merge new server messages into existing state without flash/flicker
5. Handle conversation switches gracefully (stop old poll, start new)

## Out of Scope (Future)
- WebSocket/SSE real-time push (would replace polling)
- Cross-conversation sync (only sync active conversation)
- Message editing sync

title: Bug: Project chat messages disappear because reload fetches from wrong server
description:
## Root Cause Found

The project gateway path (`sendProjectMessageViaGateway`) stores messages on a **remote bot server**, but `reloadMessagesFromServer` fetches from the **local Letta server API**.

### The Flow

1. User sends message in project chat
2. `sendProjectMessageViaGateway` → `BotGateway.routeMessage` → `RemoteBotSession.sendToAgent`
3. Message goes to **remote bot server** (e.g., LettaBot backend)
4. Response comes back, UI shows the message (lines 548-554)
5. `reloadMessagesFromServer(convId)` called (line 555)
6. Fetches from `messageRepository.fetchMessages` → `messageApi.listMessages` → **local Letta server**
7. Local server doesn't have project chat messages → returns old/empty
8. Merge logic replaces UI with empty/old → **messages disappear**

### Code Path

```kotlin
// ChatViewModel.kt line 520-523
val stream = if (projectContext != null) {
    sendProjectMessageViaGateway(text = text, conversationId = convId)  // → REMOTE
} else {
    messageRepository.sendMessage(agentId, text, convId)  // → LOCAL
}

// Line 555 - called for BOTH paths but only works for local
reloadMessagesFromServer(convId)
```

### Why It Works Sometimes
If using vanilla Letta (no projectContext), messages go to local server and reload works correctly.

### Solution Options

**Option A: Skip reload for project chat (Quick fix)**
```kotlin
is StreamState.Complete -> {
    // ...update UI...
    if (projectContext == null) {
        reloadMessagesFromServer(convId)  // Only reload for vanilla path
    }
    // ...
}
```

**Option B: Have gateway persist to local conversation (Better but more work)**
After gateway response, also persist user+assistant messages to local conversation:
```kotlin
// After getting gateway response, also save locally
messageRepository.persistLocalMessages(convId, userMessage, assistantMessage)
```

**Option C: Gateway-specific reload (Most correct)**
Create a separate reload that fetches from the bot server for project chats.

### Recommended Fix
Option A is the quickest. The project gateway should eventually have its own message persistence strategy (Option B or C), but for now, just skip reload for project chat.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Messages don't disappear after sending in project chat
- [ ] Vanilla Letta chat still works correctly (reload preserved)
- [ ] Consider long-term solution for project chat message persistence
close_reason: Fixed in commit 1c9b11b - skip local reload after project chat

title: Verify Letta tool-result continuation semantics for embedded bot runtime
description:
During bz40.1.6 implementation we added local tool execution plus tool-result submission back into the Letta conversation loop, but the exact server-side continuation semantics are not yet verified against a live Letta backend. We need to confirm whether submitting an approval/tool result via the current conversation message path is sufficient to resume the run, or whether an additional streaming/resume step is required.

Scope:
1. Validate the exact request shape for submitting local tool results back to Letta.
2. Confirm whether approval/tool-return messages automatically continue the run or require a second streamed request.
3. Add an integration test or documented manual verification path.
4. Adjust DefaultLettaRuntimeClient if the current submission approach is incomplete.

Concrete examples:
- A streamed tool call requests get_battery_status and the embedded bot executes it locally, submits the result, and receives a continued assistant response in the same logical turn.
- A multi-tool chain performs two local tools back-to-back without dropping the conversation run.
- An unavailable tool submits an error payload and the agent still recovers coherently.

This bead exists because compile-time implementation can proceed, but live continuation semantics against Letta are still a real architectural risk.

title: Runtime memory snapshots and context compression
description:
Build the memory subsystem for the embedded bot runtime, using hermes-agent as the main reference for behavior rather than copying lettabot blindly.

Scope:
1. Per-turn memory snapshots that are frozen while a turn is executing.
2. Bounded memory storage appropriate for mobile constraints.
3. Context compression and summary rollover for long conversations.
4. Memory-provider abstraction so future external memory backends can plug in without rewriting LocalBotSession.
5. Security scanning for suspicious or prompt-injecting recalled content before it is merged into runtime context.

Concrete examples:
- An agent remembers the user's preferred notification-summary style without reloading the entire conversation history each time.
- A long-running chat is compacted into a structured summary with recent turns preserved, so the next message still fits comfortably on-device.
- Mid-session memory writes update persisted state but do not mutate the already-built prompt snapshot for the active turn.

This bead should produce reusable memory abstractions consumed by LocalBotSession instead of embedding memory behavior directly into session code.
notes:
2026-04-17 triage: bead is marked in_progress but a source survey turned up ZERO implementation — no MemorySnapshot, BotMemory, contextCompression, summaryRollover, or memoryProvider types exist in bot/. Notes field is empty. This is genuinely unstarted, not 'nearly done'. Keeping in_progress is misleading; consider reverting to open until a real design pass begins. Scope is still valid — per-turn snapshots + bounded storage + compression + memory-provider abstraction + prompt-injection scanning remain the right shape for the embedded bot runtime. Dependency hint: likely wants to land after bz40.1.5 (API/service hardening — NOW CLOSED) provides a stable runtime boundary to persist against.
HANDOFF 2026-05-02: Next ready bead after share-to-agent fix. Repo is clean and pushed at git commit 7477d474 (main == origin/main). Beads are pushed. Last user-confirmed fix: Android share-to-agent now sends once and shows thinking indicator; related commits 98715215 and 7477d474.

Suggested start for this bead:
1. Treat this memory-snapshot/context-compression task as unstarted. Existing notes saying no MemorySnapshot/BotMemory/contextCompression types were found are still accurate as of quick survey.
2. Begin with design + tests, not plumbing directly into LocalBotSession. Create reusable abstractions in android-compose/bot, likely under a new memory package, then inject into LocalBotSession after interfaces are stable.
3. Relevant current files: bot/core/LocalBotSession.kt (envelope/send orchestration), bot/message/MessageEnvelopeFormatter.kt (current context injection), bot/context/* (device context provider pattern), bot/config/BotConfig*.kt (DataStore patterns), bot/di/BotModule.kt (Hilt bindings), bot/runtime/* (runtime client boundary).
4. Proposed first regression tests: per-turn snapshot remains immutable while memory writes happen mid-turn; bounded store evicts/rolls over old entries; compression preserves recent turns plus structured summary; suspicious recalled content is flagged/excluded before prompt merge.
5. Implementation guidance: keep memory provider abstraction separate from Android storage details; prefer deterministic pure unit tests for snapshot/compression/security scanner before DataStore/Hilt integration; avoid embedding memory logic directly inside LocalBotSession.

Verification baseline from previous session: :app:testRootDebugUnitTest --tests AdminChatViewModelTest passed, :app:assembleRootDebug passed, rootDebug installed on Pixel 9 Pro 192.168.50.235:5555.
CORRECTION 2026-05-02: User explicitly does NOT want runtime memory next. Do not treat the prior handoff here as active next-session direction. Next focus is durable screen-off streams/agent runs, tracked by letta-mobile-etc1 and persistent-stream epic letta-mobile-jmzq.
close_reason: Invalid: memory is handled server-side by Letta. The Android client communicates with Letta via bot protocol/WebSocket — no memory management needed on the client. Will revisit when setting up an actual embedded runtime that lives on-device.

title: Protocol compatibility layer — unified client for internal/external lettabot servers
description:
Build the protocol compatibility layer that lets the frontend talk to either the embedded bot or an external lettabot server through the same contract.

This is the central product requirement: local and remote bot backends must be interchangeable.

Scope:
1. Shared bot protocol models for /api/v1/chat, /api/v1/status, /api/v1/agents, and streaming equivalents.
2. Internal transport client that can call the embedded runtime in-process without HTTP overhead.
3. External transport client that speaks HTTP/SSE to remote lettabot servers.
4. Factory/wiring so UI and other app layers depend on one abstraction instead of branching on local vs remote everywhere.

Concrete examples:
- Chat screen sends a message through BotClient and gets chunks back; whether those chunks came from BotGateway directly or an HTTP SSE connection is invisible to the caller.
- A settings screen lists active agents from the embedded server on the phone today, then from a remote LAN server tomorrow, using the same repository interface.
- A test can validate protocol behavior locally against in-process transport before running slower networked tests against a real remote server.

This bead should define the protocol surface first, then force BotApiServer and RemoteBotSession to use that same shape.

title: Client-side tool execution framework
description:
Implement the client-side tool execution framework for the embedded bot runtime.

This should follow hermes-agent's ToolRegistry pattern more than lettabot's ad hoc tool handling: a central registry, structured tool definitions, execution policy, and explicit routing of tool results back into the active turn.

Scope:
1. Tool registry: map tool names to definitions and handlers.
2. Tool execution contract: structured args in, structured result/error out.
3. Execution policy: mark which tools are safe to run in parallel, which must never run concurrently, and which are scoped to a shared resource.
4. Permission and availability checks: a tool should be able to report "unavailable" when the device lacks permission or capability.
5. Tool-result sanitation: scan tool-provided text before feeding it back to the model/runtime.

Concrete examples:
- battery_status -> reads BatteryManager-backed state and returns a normalized JSON payload.
- open_app(packageName) -> launches an intent only if the package exists and the action is allowed.
- clipboard_read -> returns clipboard contents only when the permission and Android version rules allow it.
- notifications_list -> returns a filtered view of current notifications instead of raw unbounded dumps.

Deliverables:
- ToolRegistry / ToolHandler / ToolExecutionResult abstractions
- first batch of local Android-capable tools
- clean integration back into LocalBotSession turn processing

title: Build project agent chat — scoped chat UI connected to PM agent
description: Build the project agent chat screen. When a user taps a project tile, open a chat interface connected to that project's PM agent (letta_agent_id from VibSync). This reuses the existing chat infrastructure (ChatViewModel, ChatScreen, LettaChatClient) but scoped to the specific project agent. The chat header should show the project name and a collapsible info panel with: project path, git URL, active coding agents (if detectable), last sync time. The PM agent already has project context in its memory — the chat is just a mobile-native UI on top of the existing agent. Route messages through the embedded LettaBot client gateway (bz40.1) so the LettaBot middleware handles skill routing, memory, etc.
notes: Routed project-scoped chat sends through the embedded LettaBot gateway via ChatViewModel + InternalBotClient. Project chat now requires an enabled embedded bot config for the selected agent and surfaces a clear setup error instead of silently using the direct message API. Added focused ChatViewModel coverage for gateway sends and missing bot configuration. Verified with :app:compileDebugKotlin and :app:testDebugUnitTest --tests com.letta.mobile.ui.screens.chat.ChatViewModelTest after Kotlin cache recovery (gradlew --stop, pkill -f kotlin-daemon, gradlew cleanKotlinIC).
close_reason: Built project-scoped PM-agent chat on the existing chat UI, routed project sends through the embedded LettaBot gateway, added gateway error handling, and verified focused compile + chat tests.

title: Build project grid home screen — LazyVerticalGrid with project tiles
description: Build the project grid home screen. This replaces the current agent list as the app's landing page. Layout: LazyVerticalGrid with adaptive columns (2 on phone, 3-4 on tablet). Each tile uses M3 FilledCard (not CardGroup — CardGroup is for grouped list items, not grid tiles) with containerColor = surfaceContainerLow, RoundedCornerShape(16.dp), and tonalElevation. Each tile shows: project icon/avatar (generated from name initials or custom), project name, status indicator (active/archived), issue count badge, last activity timestamp. Tap opens project agent chat. Long-press shows ActionSheet (not context menu) for archive/delete/edit. FAB for 'New Project'. Pull-to-refresh syncs from VibSync API. EmptyState component for onboarding. Must use LettaTopBarDefaults for top bar, TypeHierarchy tokens for text, and follow the reusable screen scaffold pattern (iqwq) once available. This screen is the first adopter of the design system guardrails.
close_reason: Built the project grid landing screen, wired HomeRoute to project chat, added strings, and verified compile + focused ViewModel coverage.

title: Define project data contract — VibSync API + Kotlin data model
description: Define the data contract between the Android app and the VibSync project database. VibSync stores projects in SQLite (huly-vibe-sync.db) with schema: projects(identifier, name, huly_id, vibe_id, filesystem_path, git_url, issue_count, status, letta_agent_id, letta_folder_id, letta_source_id, description_hash, created_at, updated_at). The Android app needs to read this data. Options: (A) Direct API — VibSync exposes a REST endpoint the app queries. (B) Letta-mediated — the PM agent has project data in its memory blocks, app reads via Letta API. (C) Sync database — replicate the projects table to a local Room database on Android. Recommendation: Option A with B as enrichment. Build a lightweight ProjectsApi in VibSync that returns project list + metadata. The PM agent provides the living brief and conversational interface. Define Kotlin data classes: Project(identifier, name, path, gitUrl, status, lettaAgentId, issueCount, updatedAt).

title: [epic] Project management home screen and project agent chat
description: Build the project management home screen and project agent chat. This is the primary user-facing surface of the app. Home screen shows a grid of project tiles (sourced from VibSync project database). Tapping a project opens a project agent chat — a conversational interface backed by the project's PM agent (letta_agent_id from VibSync). The chat shows active coding agents, project brief, and supports structured bug reporting. Users can also create new projects from this screen, either by filling fields manually or iteratively through conversation with a PM agent.

title: Implement LocalBotSession core logic — agent runtime replacing letta-code
description:
Rebuild LocalBotSession as the local runtime coordinator that replaces letta-code behavior inside the Android app.

Today LocalBotSession is only a thin shell: it formats text, calls MessageApi.sendMessage(), and parses directives. The real target is an orchestrator over conversation routing, runtime memory, stream assembly, and tool dispatch.

Scope:
1. Conversation routing: keep the existing PER_CHAT / PER_CHANNEL / SHARED semantics, but move route resolution and cache management into a reusable conversation router.
2. Runtime message pipeline: format envelope, snapshot memory/context, call the Letta runtime client, process stream events, dispatch tool calls, and assemble final BotResponse.
3. Progressive streaming: expose BotResponseChunk events while a turn is running instead of waiting for the final message. Include directive/tool/thinking events where useful.
4. Serial execution and turn budget: preserve per-agent serial processing while allowing distinct conversations to remain understandable. Add an iteration budget so runaway tool loops are cut off safely.
5. Failure handling: reconnect/retry rules for interrupted streams, recoverable tool failures, and terminal errors that should surface to UI/channels.

Concrete examples:
- User says "what's my battery and network status?" -> LocalBotSession formats the message with device context, receives a tool call from Letta, dispatches to registered Android tools, sends tool result back, then streams the final answer.
- User sends two messages quickly in the same chat -> MessageQueue preserves order so the second message does not race the first turn.
- Long conversation exceeds comfortable mobile context size -> runtime loads a compressed snapshot and continues with head/tail preservation instead of blindly resending the full history.

This bead should leave LocalBotSession thin and compositional rather than turning it into one giant class.

title: Letta API client layer in :bot — conversations, streaming, memory, tools
description:
Build a bot-specific Letta runtime client layer inside the :bot module. This replaces the role that letta-code's @letta-ai/letta-client SDK played, but shaped for the embedded Kotlin runtime rather than the UI.

This layer should not just reuse the app's screen-oriented MessageApi directly. The runtime needs lower-level operations and streaming control that match bot execution semantics.

Scope:
1. Conversation lifecycle primitives: create conversation, reuse conversation, inspect conversation state when needed.
2. Streaming message send: expose a stream-oriented API that can surface assistant deltas, tool-call events, reasoning blocks, and terminal usage data.
3. Memory operations: read/update core memory blocks, create and search archival memory where the Letta API supports it, and expose attach/detach operations cleanly to runtime code.
4. Tool metadata registration: send client-side tool definitions or capabilities to the Letta server in the format the runtime needs.
5. Bot-oriented error model: transport failures, stream interruptions, auth errors, and retryable vs fatal failures should be explicit.

Concrete examples:
- Local runtime sends a user message and receives a stream: assistant delta -> tool_call(battery_status) -> tool result submission -> assistant final text.
- On session bootstrap, runtime reads the persona/human memory blocks for the target agent and snapshots them for the turn.
- When the user switches from cloud Letta to a self-hosted Letta server, this client layer swaps base URL/token without the rest of the runtime changing shape.

Deliverables:
- bot-specific Letta client interfaces and DTOs
- streaming-capable transport wrappers
- clear boundary between :core UI clients and :bot runtime clients

title: Decide LettaBot Android runtime strategy — embedded JS vs Kotlin port vs localhost bridge
description: Evaluate and decide the runtime strategy for running LettaBot's TypeScript core on Android. Options: (A) Embedded JS engine — run lettabot Node.js code via embedded V8 (Mozilla's engine) or QuickJS. Pros: reuse existing TS code directly. Cons: complex native bridging, JS↔Kotlin IPC overhead, potential memory issues. (B) Kotlin port of core abstractions — rewrite LettaGateway, AgentSession, ChannelAdapter interfaces in Kotlin. The gateway.ts is ~130 lines, interfaces.ts is ~90 lines, the channel adapter contract is ~35 lines. This is very portable. Pros: native performance, tight Compose integration, type safety. Cons: ongoing parity maintenance with upstream lettabot. (C) Localhost HTTP bridge — run a minimal lettabot Node.js process as an Android Service, communicate via localhost HTTP (the API server already exists on port configurable). Pros: near-zero porting. Cons: process management complexity, battery impact. Recommendation: Option B (Kotlin port) for the stripped-down single-gateway version. The core interfaces are small and stable.
close_reason: Decision made: Kotlin port. Confirmed after full investigation of lettabot (54K lines TS), letta-code (coding harness delegating to Letta server API), and existing :bot module (21 files, ~1869 lines with correct skeletal architecture). Kotlin wins because: existing skeleton covers ~60% of structure, zero JNI interop tax, coroutines map perfectly to I/O-bound orchestration, Ktor handles both client and server sides. letta-code subprocess model disappears — LocalBotSession calls Letta server API directly via Ktor client.

title: [epic] Embedded LettaBot server architecture for Android
description:
Kotlin rewrite of the embedded LettaBot server for Android. The existing :bot module already has the outer lettabot-shaped scaffolding (gateway, sessions, channels, config, service, API server, DI, context providers, message queue, directive parser, envelope formatter). The remaining work is to turn that skeleton into a real local agent runtime.

Architectural split for this epic:
1. lettabot defines the protocol and surface area: BotGateway, BotSession, channel adapters, /api/v1/chat, /api/v1/status, /api/v1/agents, conversation routing, directives.
2. hermes-agent defines the runtime internals: tool registry and dispatch, bounded memory, context compression, iteration budgeting, progressive stream consumption, cron semantics.
3. hermes-android defines Android-native execution patterns: permission-aware device tools, long-lived service behavior, WebSocket/device bridge ideas, accessibility-backed action boundaries.

The biggest current gap is that LocalBotSession is not yet a true local lettabot runtime. It still formats a message and calls the app's MessageApi directly. It must become an orchestrator over a bot-specific Letta runtime client, runtime memory, stream assembly, and tool dispatch.

Examples of the target architecture:
- In-app chat sends "summarize my unread notifications" -> BotGateway routes to LocalBotSession -> runtime streams Letta response -> tool registry invokes notification-reading tool -> final answer returns through the same bot protocol used by remote servers.
- Another screen points at an external lettabot server instead of the embedded one -> the frontend still talks to the same /api/v1 contract and does not care whether transport is in-process or HTTP.
- A scheduled daily check-in runs through WorkManager -> heartbeat/cron injects a synthetic message into the same LocalBotSession pipeline instead of using a separate scheduling-specific code path.

Primary implementation tracks under this epic:
- protocol compatibility layer for internal/external servers
- bot-specific Letta runtime client
- LocalBotSession runtime orchestration
- tool registry and Android tool adapters
- runtime memory and context compression
- foreground service and API hardening
- heartbeat/cron scheduling
- multi-server profile management
- skills as prompt/tool bundles

title: [epic] LettaBot Android Client — embedded server + project management
description: Transform Letta Mobile from a direct Letta API admin tool into a full LettaBot client with project management. Two major capabilities: (1) Embedded LettaBot client — a stripped-down LettaBot server running on Android with a single gateway (the app itself), connecting to a remote Letta API. Supports multi-server selection so you can hot-swap Letta backends. (2) Project management home screen — grid of project tiles sourced from VibSync project database, each opening a project agent chat that shows active agents, living project brief, structured bug reporting. The existing admin screens remain as a secondary 'under the hood' layer for troubleshooting. Data model source: huly-vibe-sync ProjectRepository (projects table: identifier, name, filesystem_path, git_url, letta_agent_id, letta_folder_id, status).

title: Remove all visible outlines — tonal fills only, no strokes anywhere
description: Audit every screen for remaining visible borders/outlines on cards, list items, and containers. RikkaHub achieves visual hierarchy purely through tonal surface differences and spacing — no strokes, no outlines, no borderColor. Search codebase for: BorderStroke, border(), OutlinedCard, borderColor, outlineVariant usage. Replace all visible borders with tonal surface fills. Cards should use CustomColors.cardColors (containerColor = surfaceContainer). List items should use CustomColors.listItemColors (containerColor = surfaceBright). This is the single highest-impact visual change.

title: Align CustomColors with Material 3 tonal palette — derive from colorScheme
description: CustomColors has hardcoded hex values that don't fully leverage Material 3's tonal palette system. RikkaHub derives all custom colors from the dynamic MaterialTheme.colorScheme (surfaceContainer, surfaceBright, primary, etc.) rather than specifying absolute colors. Audit CustomColors: replace hardcoded hex values with references to MaterialTheme.colorScheme tokens where possible. agentBubbleBgColor should be colorScheme.surfaceContainerLow, toolBubbleBgColor should be colorScheme.secondaryContainer, etc. This ensures the app respects dynamic color (Material You) and preset themes correctly. Reference: RikkaHub's CustomColors object in Color.kt which derives cardColors, listItemColors, and topBarColors from colorScheme.

title: Extract LettaChatClient — owns SSE, streaming, message ordering
description:
The core of the module. Owns the chat protocol.

LettaChatClient:
- connect(agentId, conversationId?) → resolves or creates conversation
- sendMessage(text) → returns Flow<StreamEvent>
- messages: StateFlow<List<ChatMessage>> — always ordered, deduped
- conversation: StateFlow<ChatConversation?>
- isStreaming: StateFlow<Boolean>

Handles:
- Conversation resolution (find most recent or create)
- User message added immediately to list
- Stream response appended (not replaced)
- Message deduplication by ID
- Auto-set conversation summary from first message

- :app owns: ViewModel, navigation, screen scaffolding
- :chat depends on: :core (API layer), :designsystem (theme)
- :chat does NOT depend on: :app

Benefits:
1. Testable in isolation (no Android framework deps for logic)

4. Clear ownership boundary

title: Remove blocking cache hydration from AgentRepository startup path
description:
## Why
core/src/main/java/com/letta/mobile/data/repository/AgentRepository.kt uses runBlocking in the singleton init path to read Room cache synchronously. This can block app startup and introduce visible cold-start jank.

## Scope
- Remove synchronous cache loading from AgentRepository init
- Preserve the current behavior of showing cached agents quickly when available
- Ensure startup remains safe if Room read fails

## Files
- android-compose/core/src/main/java/com/letta/mobile/data/repository/AgentRepository.kt
- Any DI/coroutine-scope wiring needed to support async hydration

## Deliverables
- Cache hydration happens asynchronously
- No runBlocking remains in repository initialization
- Agent list consumers still receive cached data once loaded
design: Use asynchronous repository initialization rather than synchronous DI-time reads. Prefer a repository-owned coroutine scope or injected application scope over ad hoc blocking calls.
acceptance_criteria:
- AgentRepository no longer imports or calls runBlocking
- Cached agents are loaded off the main thread
- Failure to hydrate cache logs safely without blocking startup
- Existing agent list behavior remains correct with and without cached data
notes: Implemented: AgentRepository cache hydration now runs asynchronously on an IO-backed repository scope; runBlocking removed. Oracle review judged the change safe because all consumers already tolerate an initial empty cache window. Verification in this environment is blocked by unaccepted Android SDK licenses for build-tools 34/platform 35, so Gradle compile/test could not complete here.
