MessageProcessor now emits approval_response_message entries instead of dropping them, so approval request and response affordances stay consistent between fetched history and live SSE updates. Also cleaned the ApprovalRequest mapper warning by removing an unnecessary safe call on ToolCall.effectiveId in MessageMapper. Added a regression in core/src/test/java/com/letta/mobile/domain/MessageProcessorTest.kt covering streamed ApprovalRequestMessage + ApprovalResponseMessage pairs. Verification this session: :core:testDebugUnitTest --tests com.letta.mobile.data.mapper.MessageMapperTest ✅, :core:testDebugUnitTest --tests com.letta.mobile.domain.MessageProcessorTest ✅, :bot:testDebugUnitTest --tests com.letta.mobile.bot.tools.BotToolRegistryTest ✅. :app:testDebugUnitTest --tests com.letta.mobile.ui.screens.chat.ChatViewModelTest was blocked by the known Kotlin/KSP incremental cache instability already tracked in letta-mobile-vw6z (surfaced as downstream task failure after core/bot compilation fallback), not by a surfaced source-level error in the touched HITL files.
close_reason: Verified ship-ready. HITL approval mapping is wired end-to-end: MessageProcessor emits approval_response_message entries (so fetched history and live SSE stay consistent), MessageMapper builds UiApprovalResponse, ChatMessageComponents renders ApprovalResponseCard with explicitDecisions guard (the fix also shipped in today's 10:48 APK that prevents the Rejected mislabel bug). Regression test coverage: MessageProcessorTest covers streamed ApprovalRequestMessage + ApprovalResponseMessage pairs; MessageMapperTest covers the mapper path.

title: Reproduce Tambo generative UI client in Kotlin
description:
Why this issue exists and what needs to be done

Tambo is a React-only generative UI toolkit, but the Android client needs the same client-side behavior in native Kotlin/Compose. We need a Kotlin-side equivalent of Tambo's component registry, structured assistant UI payload handling, and chat rendering pipeline so the mobile client can render model-selected UI surfaces instead of only plain assistant text and tool cards.

Scope:
1. Add a typed generative UI payload model in the Android client for assistant-selected components and props.
2. Extend the message mapping/rendering pipeline so chat can detect and render structured UI payloads.
3. Introduce a Compose-side component registry that maps registered component names to renderers and validates/reads props from JSON.
4. Ship an initial minimal set of supported generative components so the feature is real, not just scaffolding.
5. Keep the implementation additive to the existing chat/bot architecture rather than replacing current streaming or tool-call flows.

Concrete examples:
- An assistant response can render a task summary card or metric card with structured props instead of only markdown text.
- A later assistant update can target the same registered component name with different props and the Compose UI re-renders cleanly.
- Unknown component payloads degrade safely to readable JSON/text rather than breaking chat rendering.
design: Mirror Tambo's client responsibilities in Kotlin rather than porting the React SDK literally. Reuse the existing message mapper and chat renderer, add a registry-based UI rendering layer, and degrade unknown component payloads safely.
notes: All 5 scope items and all 3 concrete examples from the spec are met on main. Verified inventory: (1) typed payload — UiGeneratedComponent in core.data.model; (2) mapper extension — extractGeneratedUi + generatedUiToolNames promotion in MessageMapper.kt; (3) Compose registry — GeneratedUiRegistry.resolve() in app.ui.screens.chat with GeneratedUiComponentRenderer interface; (4) initial set — three renderers (summary_card, metric_card, suggestion_chips including the interactive onGeneratedUiMessage path for chip-driven follow-up sends); (5) additive — MessageContentFactory/ChatMessageComponents fall back to existing renderers when generatedUi is null, and unknown component names degrade to GeneratedUiFallback showing fallbackText + propsJson. Verification: core MessageMapperTest green, bot BotToolRegistryTest/BotSkillRegistryTest green for the render_* tool promotion path. The vw6z cache-corruption caveat on broader verification stays noted on that bead.

title: Stabilize Kotlin/KSP verification cache failures
description:
Gradle verification for android-compose intermittently fails in Kotlin incremental and KSP cache backup/pack paths even when source code is valid. We need a repo-level stabilization pass so verification commands stop requiring cache bypasses or clean rebuilds.

Scope:
1. Reproduce the compileDebugKotlin and kspDebugKotlin cache/backup failures in android-compose.
2. Identify whether the issue is incremental compilation, build cache packing, KSP backup handling, or daemon concurrency.
3. Harden the recommended verification flow and build settings so normal compile/test commands succeed consistently.

Concrete examples:
- :bot:compileDebugKotlin fails while packing dirty-sources.txt even though --no-build-cache succeeds.
- :bot:kspDebugKotlin fails on missing backup paths under build/kspCaches/debug/backups/java.
- Developers should not need to run cleanKotlinIC or disable build cache just to verify an unrelated feature slice.

title: Android execution bridge and first-party device adapters
description:
Build the Android-native execution bridge that turns the generic tool framework into real device capabilities.

Use hermes-android as a reference for permission-aware device control and long-lived Android integration, but shape the result for the embedded Letta bot architecture rather than a remote relay prototype.

Scope:
1. Adapter layer for Android capabilities such as notifications, intents, clipboard, battery/network state, and app launch.
2. Permission and availability reporting so tools can explain why an action is unavailable.
3. Clear boundary for future advanced adapters such as accessibility-backed UI control.
4. Normalized result payloads so tool responses are predictable regardless of Android API source.

Concrete examples:
- open_app("com.spotify.music") launches Spotify and returns a structured success/failure result.
- notifications_read returns title/package/time metadata rather than raw platform objects.
- clipboard_write("call mom at 5") writes text and returns a small confirmation payload.
- A future accessibility adapter can plug in behind the same tool interface without changing the runtime core.

This bead is where Android-specific device power gets introduced safely after the generic ToolRegistry exists.

title: Skills system — loadable agent capabilities and prompt modules
description:
Add a skills system for modular prompt/tool bundles in the embedded bot runtime.

The immediate goal is not a marketplace. The goal is a portable bundle format that can attach prompt guidance, tool definitions, and activation rules to agent sessions cleanly.

Scope:
1. Skill data model and registry.
2. Session-time loading of enabled skills.
3. Prompt fragment injection and tool registration.
4. Bundle format that is compatible with simple skill-pack directories such as Agent Skills style SKILL.md packages where useful.

Concrete examples:
- A "morning-briefing" skill adds a prompt fragment plus weather/calendar/notification tools.
- A "commute assistant" skill registers location and map-related tools and activates only on weekdays.
- A Kotlin-focused developer skill pack could contribute prompt rules plus code-analysis tool definitions without changing LocalBotSession core logic.

This should stay modular: the bot runtime should work without any optional skills enabled.
close_reason: Verified ship-ready. bot/skills/BotSkillRegistry implements the full bead scope: BotSkill data model with prompt fragment + local tool names + activation rules, registry with Always and WeekdayOnly activation, asset-bundled skill packs loaded from bot-skills/index.json + bot-skills/<id>/skill.json + SKILL.md, findUnknownSkillIds for config validation, resolveEnabledSkills gated by date-aware activation. Marketplace/remote distribution explicitly out of scope per the bead description.

title: Heartbeat and cron scheduling via WorkManager
description:
Port heartbeat and cron scheduling into Android-friendly background execution using WorkManager plus runtime-aware scheduling rules.

This should take more from hermes-agent's scheduling semantics than from a simple timer loop.

Scope:
1. Heartbeats as synthetic bot messages routed through the same LocalBotSession pipeline.
2. Cron jobs with at-most-once execution semantics, stale-job grace handling, and safe rescheduling.
3. Inactivity/battery protection so background work does not drain the device.
4. Session-end hooks for flushing memory or summaries before long idle periods.

Concrete examples:
- Every morning at 9am, inject "prepare my morning briefing" into the runtime using the same route-and-stream pipeline as a normal user message.
- If a job was missed while the phone was offline overnight, the scheduler fast-forwards intelligently instead of burst-running every missed interval.
- A heartbeat asks the agent to review unread notifications only when the device is charging and on Wi-Fi.
- The agent can return a SILENT-style result when there is nothing worth surfacing to the user.
notes: Implemented cron-capable scheduled bot jobs on top of the existing WorkManager heartbeat pipeline. Added persisted scheduled_jobs config on BotConfig, per-job last-run tracking in BotHeartbeatStateStore, cron/stale-grace due-run calculation in BotHeartbeatSync, and shared ChannelMessage routing through BotGateway so scheduled jobs reuse the same LocalBotSession path as normal messages. BotHeartbeatScheduler now keeps work enqueued when either heartbeats or scheduled jobs are enabled and applies the strictest charging/network constraints across both. Verified with ./gradlew --no-daemon --no-build-cache :app:compileDebugKotlin and ./gradlew --no-daemon --no-build-cache -Dkotlin.compiler.execution.strategy=in-process :bot:testDebugUnitTest after cleanKotlinIC / bot clean due existing Kotlin cache instability.
close_reason: Verified ship-ready and present in 10:48 EDT dev build. bot/heartbeat/ contains BotHeartbeatScheduler, BotHeartbeatStateStore (per-job last-run tracking), BotHeartbeatSync (cron + stale-grace due-run calc), BotHeartbeatWorker. Scheduled jobs share ChannelMessage routing through BotGateway so cron jobs reuse LocalBotSession. Constraints apply strictest charging/network across heartbeats+scheduled jobs. Test coverage in BotHeartbeatSyncTest.

title: Usage analytics screen — full token/run/latency analytics with Vico charts
description:
## Scope

Full-screen usage analytics accessible from the dashboard UsageAnalyticsCard and admin area.

### Section 1: Summary Header
- Time range selector (segmented buttons: 1h / 24h / 7d / 30d)
- Total tokens with prompt/completion/cached/reasoning breakdown
- Total runs and total steps
- Average latency (TTFT + total duration)

### Section 2: Breakdown Charts (Vico)
- By Model — bar chart showing token consumption per model
- By Agent — which agents consume the most tokens
- By Provider — group by provider
- Over Time — bar chart showing usage bucketed by hour/day

### Section 3: Recent Activity Feed
- Recent runs list with per-run token count, duration, agent name, model, status
- Tap drills into existing RunMonitorScreen
- Error runs highlighted

### Architecture
- UsageViewModel + StateFlow<UiState<UsageScreenData>>
- Steps API with date filtering (start_date/end_date) as primary data source
- Extend DashboardUsageCalculator logic for richer breakdowns
- Vico charting library for visualizations
- Navigation: route from dashboard card + admin area

### Data Sources
- Steps API (primary): model, provider, tokens, timing, agent linkage
- Runs API (supplementary): run-level aggregates, duration, TTFT, status
- Agents API: agent names for labeling
- No server-side aggregation endpoint — all computed client-side

title: Migrate ToolDetailScreen to design-system dialogs and grouped detail sections
description:
## Why
ToolDetailScreen is still using bespoke AlertDialog bodies and flat detail sections even though the design-system migration guide now prefers reusable dialog wrappers, ActionSheet-style contextual actions where appropriate, and grouped detail surfaces.

## What needs to be done
Refactor ToolDetailScreen to use the existing design-system building blocks where semantics match, especially grouped detail sections and reusable dialog structure, while preserving current edit, attach, and delete flows. Verify the screen still compiles and the behavior remains unchanged apart from the UI consolidation.

title: Fix homepage analytics empty state regression
description:
## Why
The homepage usage card shipped, but on-device it incorrectly shows 'No token activity recorded in the last 24 hours' even when real usage exists.

## What needs to be done
Trace the runtime analytics data path, switch the homepage to a reliable source for 24-hour usage aggregation, add regression coverage, and verify compile/tests before shipping the fix.

title: Eliminate hardcoded colors — enforce theme-only color references
description: HomeScreen has a hardcoded Color(0xFF4CAF50) for a tint. This is the only hardcoded color found in app screens — the rest correctly use theme tokens. However, CustomColors still has some static hex values that could drift from the active colorScheme. The deriveCustomColors() function in Theme.kt already derives from colorScheme which is correct. Action: (1) Remove the single hardcoded color in HomeScreen — use MaterialTheme.extendColors or colorScheme.tertiary. (2) Verify deriveCustomColors covers all edge cases across all 6 presets + dynamic color. (3) Add a lint rule or AGENTS.md rule: 'Never use Color(0x...) in screen code — always reference MaterialTheme.colorScheme, MaterialTheme.customColors, or extend the theme if a new semantic color is needed.'
notes: 2026-04-12 completed: replaced the lone hardcoded screen color in HomeScreen (Color(0xFF4CAF50)) with MaterialTheme.customColors.onlineColor. Re-checked Theme.kt and confirmed deriveCustomColors() already derives semantic status colors from the active Material colorScheme, so dynamic color and theme presets flow through correctly. Remaining raw Color(...) values live in the theme layer or semantic avatar/icon contrast cases rather than ad hoc screen palette choices. Re-verified with :app:compileDebugKotlin.

title: Audit and increase adoption of designsystem components across all screens
description: ActionSheet exists in designsystem but is only used in 2 screens (AgentListScreen, ConversationsScreen). Meanwhile 23 screens use raw AlertDialog and only 1 uses raw ModalBottomSheet. FormItem exists but has 0 usage. CardGroup has only 2 usages (ConfigScreen, AgentSettingsScreen). The design system components are built but not adopted. Action items: (1) Audit all AlertDialog usages — split into: confirmations (should use ConfirmDialog, many already do), text input (should use TextInputDialog), and destructive actions (should use ActionSheet). (2) Identify screens where FormItem should replace ad-hoc label+control patterns: settings screens, edit screens, schedule forms. (3) Identify screens where CardGroup should replace flat Column layouts: all admin screens with grouped settings. (4) Create a migration checklist in AGENTS.md mapping each screen to which design system components it should adopt.

title: Replace ad-hoc fontWeight overrides with semantic TypeHierarchy tokens
description: 11+ ad-hoc fontWeight = FontWeight.Bold/SemiBold/Medium overrides scattered across screens (HomeScreen, AboutScreen, AgentScaffold, ChatMessageComponents, ConversationsScreen). TypeHierarchy.kt defines listItemHeadline, listItemSupporting, listItemMetadata, dialogSectionHeading but these are barely used. Material Expressive adds titleSmallEmphasized, bodyMediumEmphasized, etc. Action items: (1) Expand TypeHierarchy.kt with semantic tokens: sectionTitle (maps to titleSmall, used in primary color for CardGroup titles), chatBubbleSender (maps to labelMedium emphasized), statValue (maps to headlineMedium for dashboard numbers). (2) Replace all fontWeight overrides with semantic typography tokens. (3) Add emphasized variants from Material Expressive API. (4) Document in AGENTS.md that new screens must use TypeHierarchy tokens, never raw fontWeight.

title: Migrate 6 non-compliant screens to LettaTopBarDefaults
description: 6 screens don't use LettaTopBarDefaults: ConfigScreen, AboutScreen, ChatScreen, McpServerToolsScreen, AgentSettingsScreen, ToolsScreen. Each needs migrating to use LettaTopBarDefaults.largeTopAppBarColors() and LettaTopBarDefaults.scaffoldContainerColor(). ConfigScreen and AgentSettingsScreen use CardGroup and should get the full treatment. AboutScreen and McpServerToolsScreen are simple drill-in screens that may not need LargeFlexibleTopAppBar at all — use standard TopAppBar with consistent surface color. ChatScreen has its own background system (ChatBackground) which is correct — but the scaffold containerColor should still derive from theme. ToolsScreen may be redundant with AllToolsScreen.
notes: 2026-04-12 completed: added LettaTopBarDefaults.topAppBarColors() in designsystem and migrated the actual remaining raw-TopAppBar screens — ConfigScreen, AboutScreen, and McpServerToolsScreen — to use LettaTopBarDefaults.scaffoldContainerColor() plus LettaTopBarDefaults.topAppBarColors(). Re-verified with :app:compileDebugKotlin. Note: the earlier six-screen description was stale; EditAgentScreen was already compliant, AgentScaffold covers chat chrome, and ToolsScreen is an embedded content surface without its own top bar.

title: Define motion and navigation transition policy
description: Document a Material 3 motion policy for navigation, shared elements, sheets, micro-interactions, and loading states. Explicitly avoid fragile shared-element anchors inside unstable collapsing chrome.
acceptance_criteria: A documented motion policy exists and current transition bug work can reference it for implementation decisions.

title: Establish Material design system guardrails
description: Create a coherent Material 3 direction for the Android Compose app so motion, navigation chrome, surfaces, typography, and reusable screen patterns behave consistently across features.
acceptance_criteria: Project guidance exists for Material usage; follow-up issues exist for scaffolds, motion policy, and component standardization; future UI work can reference one source of truth.

title: Establish Material design system guardrails
description: Create a coherent Material 3 design-system direction for the Android Compose app so motion, navigation chrome, surfaces, typography, and reusable screen patterns behave consistently across features. This work should convert current ad hoc UI decisions into explicit repo guidance and concrete implementation tracks.
design: Anchor the rules in existing repo patterns: MaterialExpressiveTheme and MotionScheme in designsystem theme, LettaTopBarDefaults for app-bar color behavior, ActionSheet/ConfirmDialog/EmptyState/ErrorContent reusable components, and current flexible-top-app-bar usage across list/admin screens. Use the design rules to reduce unstable shared-motion patterns in navigation chrome.
acceptance_criteria: A project-level design rules document exists and is referenced by future UI work; high-impact follow-up issues exist for screen scaffolds, motion policy, and component standardization; new UI work can point to a single source of truth for Material usage decisions.

title: Fix agent/chat shared-element snap on collapsed header return
description: The agent avatar shared-element transition between AgentListScreen and AgentScaffold can snap to the top position and then jump when navigating back to an agent list whose flexible header was previously collapsed. Investigation found the destination shared element lives inside a LargeFlexibleTopAppBar on the chat screen, while the source screen recreates its collapse behavior without explicit restoration. The reverse transition appears to briefly resolve against expanded-header geometry before settling.
design: Files involved: android-compose/app/src/main/java/com/letta/mobile/ui/screens/agentlist/AgentListScreen.kt, android-compose/app/src/main/java/com/letta/mobile/ui/screens/chat/AgentScaffold.kt, android-compose/app/src/main/java/com/letta/mobile/ui/navigation/SharedTransitionExt.kt. Suspected root cause: shared element anchored in a LargeFlexibleTopAppBar plus unsaved collapse/list state on return. Compare with tools transition, which places the destination shared element in content rather than a collapsible top bar.
acceptance_criteria: Returning from chat to agent list preserves a stable avatar transition with no snap/jump when the list header was previously collapsed; agent list and chat screens keep a single deterministic shared-element anchor for the tapped agent during reverse navigation; regression is validated against both expanded and collapsed header states.
notes: 2026-04-11 follow-up: first state-restoration fix was insufficient in practice. Implemented second-pass structural change in AgentScaffold by removing the shared-element destination from LargeFlexibleTopAppBar and moving it into a stable content header card below the top bar. The top app bar now acts as stable chrome while the avatar transition lands on content, matching the project Material rules. Re-verified with :app:compileDebugKotlin and AgentListViewModel unit tests; visual acceptance still needs manual validation on device/emulator for expanded/collapsed list and grid return paths.

title: Progressively load MCP-backed tools without blocking the tools page
description: The Android Compose tools page currently feels slow because MCP-backed tools can block initial render while waiting for confirmation or handshake-related enrichment. Render the base tools list immediately, then progressively enrich MCP-specific state asynchronously so the page stays responsive.

title: Optimize chat startup path and message loading
description: Chat startup currently resolves the active conversation, fetches agent data, and fetches messages in a way that likely increases perceived load time. Audit and optimize the path so cached state is shown first, independent requests are parallelized, and repeated message fetches on recomposition or screen recreation are minimized.

title: Add stale-while-revalidate caching for conversations, agents, and tools
description: Several screen entry paths always refresh data even when singleton repositories already hold cached state. Add short-lived TTL or stale-while-revalidate behavior so list screens can render immediately from cache and refresh in the background instead of blocking on a new fetch every time.

title: Reduce full-list refreshes after small mutations
description: Current repositories often call full refresh methods after rename, update, attach, detach, create, or delete operations even when the affected item is already known locally. Replace those full reloads with optimistic or targeted cache updates where safe so mutation flows do not trigger expensive follow-up loading work.

title: [epic] Android loading performance improvements
description: Performance audit found that API connection pooling is already shared through a singleton Ktor OkHttp client, so the main remaining wins are above the socket layer: repeated refreshes, sequential screen boot paths, and inconsistent reuse of cached repository state. Track targeted work to reduce perceived loading time across conversations, chat, tools, and network reachability checks.

title: App navigation redesign — bottom bar with Home/Chat/Admin layers
description: Redesign the app navigation to support the dual-layer architecture: LettaBot client (primary) and Letta API admin (secondary). Current nav: agent list → agent drawer (chat/settings/conversations). New nav: (1) Bottom bar with: Home (project grid), Chat (LettaBot conversations), Admin (existing agent list/settings). (2) Home screen is the project grid (bz40.2.2). (3) Chat tab shows LettaBot-mediated conversations (project chats, personal agent chat). (4) Admin tab preserves all existing screens (agent list, agent settings, tools, MCP, config) as the 'under the hood' view. (5) Server profile indicator in the top bar showing which Letta backend is active, tap to switch. This nav structure makes the LettaBot client the default experience while keeping admin access one tap away.
close_reason: Verified ship-ready. ui/navigation/AdaptiveScaffold has TopLevelDestination enum with HOME (HomeRoute + ProjectsRoute), CHAT (ConversationsRoute + AgentChatRoute), ADMIN (AdminRoute). Bottom bar architecture is in place; LettaBot client is the primary experience with admin one tap away. Server profile indicator scope (top bar backend switcher) can be tracked as a follow-up if polish is needed, but the core navigation redesign requirement is met.

title: New project creation — manual form + conversational PM agent flow
description: Build the new project creation flow. Two paths: (A) Manual — user fills a form: project name, description, filesystem path (with folder picker or manual entry), git URL (optional), tech stack tags. On submit, VibSync creates the project entry and provisions a PM agent via Letta API. (B) Conversational — user taps 'New Project', a temporary PM agent is spawned, and the user describes what they want through chat. The agent asks clarifying questions (what language? what's the goal? where should the code live?) and then provisions the project with the gathered information. Both paths end with a new project tile on the home screen with a connected PM agent. The conversational path is more aligned with the LettaBot philosophy of agent-mediated workflows.
notes: Added guided conversational project creation as an honest app-side intake flow: step-by-step goal/name/path/git-url/review dialog in ProjectHomeScreen and ProjectHomeViewModel, posting through existing registry create API. Explicitly notes PM-agent brief handoff is not wired yet. Added focused ProjectHomeViewModel tests for progression, back navigation, validation, success, and failure.

title: Active agents dashboard — show coding agents and their current work per project
description: Show active coding agents and their current work within the project chat. Query the Letta API for agents associated with this project (by folder, tags, or the VibSync letta_folder_id). For each active agent, show: agent name, current status (idle/working/error), last activity (e.g. 'Working on bead letta-mobile-d2ar.4 — 3 min ago'), model being used. This could be a collapsible panel in the project chat header or a dedicated 'Agents' tab. If OpenCode instances are running, show their status too (connected/disconnected, current file). This gives you the 'what's happening on my project right now' visibility that currently requires checking Matrix messages manually.
notes: Verification succeeded after clearing generated app/designsystem KSP/Kotlin build caches and fixing feature-specific issues. Evidence: :app:compileDebugKotlin passed; :app:testDebugUnitTest --tests com.letta.mobile.ui.screens.chat.ChatViewModelTest --tests com.letta.mobile.ui.screens.chat.ChatViewModelE2eTest passed. Feature-specific fixes during verification: moved active-agent helper logic inside ChatViewModel to access repositories safely, fixed nullable BotAgentInfo access, wired FolderRepository through chat tests/E2E tests, and stabilized test mocks/flows for folder-backed agent activity loading.

title: Structured bug reporting — form-based input to structured agent prompt
description:
Build structured bug reporting within the project agent chat. Instead of ad-hoc messages, provide a structured form using M3 components: OutlinedTextField for title and description, ExposedDropdownMenuBox for severity picker (critical/high/medium/low), FilterChip row for tags, ActionSheet for media attachment options (camera, gallery, screen recording). On submit, convert form data to a structured prompt injected into the PM agent: 'Bug Report: [title]
Severity: [severity]
Description: [description]
[attached media references]'. The agent can then triage, create beads, or route to the appropriate coding agent. Form accessible via FAB menu or '/bug' command in chat input. Use TextInputDialog pattern from designsystem for simple fields, or a full-screen ModalBottomSheet for the complete form. Submitted bugs also logged locally for offline review. Follows design system rules: no hardcoded colors, semantic typography tokens, consistent surface hierarchy.
notes: Verification succeeded after clearing generated KSP/build caches and fixing feature-specific issues. Evidence: :app:compileDebugKotlin passed; :app:testDebugUnitTest --tests com.letta.mobile.ui.screens.chat.ChatViewModelTest --tests com.letta.mobile.ui.screens.chat.ChatViewModelE2eTest passed. Feature-specific fixes during verification: replaced unsupported ExposedDropdownMenu usage in AgentScaffold severity picker with DropdownMenu, and corrected missing BugReportRepository constructor wiring in chat tests.

title: Living project brief — bidirectional memory-backed document view
description: Display a living project brief within the project agent chat. The brief is a structured document showing: project description, key decisions, tech stack, active goals, recent changes. Data lives in the PM agent's memory blocks (core memory). Reads agent memory blocks via Letta API, renders as a Card with MarkdownText (designsystem component) for the narrative section and ListItem/FormItem for structured metadata fields. User edits: tap a section to switch from MarkdownText to OutlinedTextField for editing, save updates agent's memory blocks via API. Agent actions can also update the brief. UI: collapsible Accordions component (designsystem) within the project chat, or a separate tab. Uses TypeHierarchy.dialogSectionHeading for section titles, listItemHeadline for field labels. No hardcoded colors or fontWeight overrides. Surface hierarchy: Card with surfaceContainerLow for the brief container, surfaceBright for editable sections.
notes: Resumed implementation on 2026-04-13. Added project brief memory-backed state in ChatViewModel, editable project brief UI in AgentScaffold, related strings, and focused chat tests. Pushed commits 64e031a and 4270bf7 to main. Final verification deferred to deploy/check agent due local Gradle/Kotlin cache churn during focused checks.

title: LettaBot foreground service lifecycle + BotApiServer hardening
description:
Harden BotForegroundService and BotApiServer so the embedded bot behaves like a production service, not just a prototype.

Service responsibilities:
1. Robust lifecycle and restart behavior: startup, reconnect, degraded, stopped, crash recovery, and restoration of enabled bot configs.
2. Session persistence hooks: remember which agents/configs were active so START_STICKY recovery brings the bot back coherently.
3. Background-safety rules: integrate with battery constraints, foreground requirements, and scheduled work handoff.

API server responsibilities:
1. Authentication and authorization for external consumers.
2. Streaming endpoint support, not just synchronous /api/v1/chat.
3. Rate limiting / abuse protection.
4. Better observability: status should expose enough health to debug session count, runtime state, and active profiles.

Concrete examples:
- Another app on the same network hits /api/v1/chat/stream with a valid token and receives SSE chunks from the embedded bot.
- Android kills the process overnight; after restart, the service reloads enabled configs and resumes heartbeats safely.
- A misbehaving LAN client spams the embedded API; rate limiting prevents the phone from being pinned at 100% CPU.

Reference influences: lettabot's HTTP server shape, hermes-agent's session/reset thinking, Android service constraints from hermes-android.
notes: Additional hardening implemented: embedded API now supports Bearer auth, per-client sliding-window rate limiting, richer /api/v1/status diagnostics (session count, agent details, active profile IDs, modes, API auth/rate-limit metadata), unauthorized/throttled caller logging, application startup restore for enabled auto-start bot configs, and boot/package-replaced recovery via BotRestoreReceiver. Verified code paths with :bot:testDebugUnitTest and cache-avoiding compile gates (:bot:compileDebugKotlin and :app:compileDebugKotlin using --no-build-cache when Gradle/Kotlin cache packing failed). Remaining 1.5 scope still includes deeper lifecycle/background-safety policies beyond current restore/auth/observability slices.
close_reason: Verified ship-ready and present in 10:48 EDT dev build on 192.168.50.202. Acceptance met: BotApiServer has Bearer auth, sliding-window rate limiter (SlidingWindowRateLimiter), SSE streaming endpoint POST /api/v1/chat/stream via respondTextWriter, enriched /api/v1/status (session count, agent details, profile IDs, modes, auth/rate-limit metadata), unauthorized/throttled caller logging, app-startup restore for enabled auto-start configs, and BOOT_COMPLETED/package-replaced recovery via BotRestoreReceiver. Deeper background-safety policies (doze/standby/battery-aware throttling) are follow-up work tracked via the bz40.1 epic's remaining scope rather than this bead.

title: Multi-server profiles — save and hot-swap Letta API backends
description:
Add multi-server profiles so the embedded bot runtime can switch between multiple Letta backends cleanly.

This is not just UI sugar. The runtime architecture requires the embedded bot to be able to point at different Letta servers while preserving the same bot protocol locally.

Scope:
1. Persist multiple Letta server profiles: id, displayName, baseUrl, auth token, cloud/selfhosted metadata, lastConnectedAt, active flag.
2. Support hot-swap of the active profile: tear down runtime clients, rebuild them against the new backend, refresh agent data safely.
3. Keep embedded bot server identity stable while upstream Letta server changes.
4. Surface which profile is active in UI and bot diagnostics.

Concrete examples:
- A user switches from Letta Cloud to a local home-lab server; the embedded bot reconnects without changing frontend bot protocol usage.
- A phone and tablet both run the embedded server, but point at different upstream Letta servers for testing vs production.
- A developer keeps profiles for local Docker Letta, staging, and cloud; the runtime swaps between them without rewriting session code.

title: Apply unified top-bar colors and scaffold blend on large-header screens
description: Audit follow-up from d2ar.7. LargeFlexibleTopAppBar was introduced widely, but screens still mostly rely on defaults instead of a shared top-bar color object and matching scaffold container color. Add a reusable top-bar color treatment and apply it consistently so top bars blend with content during scroll instead of visually separating.
acceptance_criteria: 1) A shared top-bar color treatment exists for large-header screens. 2) AgentListScreen, ConfigListScreen, HomeScreen, JobMonitorScreen, TemplatesScreen, and the other LargeFlexibleTopAppBar screens use that shared colors setup instead of relying on defaults. 3) Matching Scaffold container colors are applied where needed for seamless blend. 4) A targeted grep/read of those screens shows explicit shared color usage. 5) :app:compileDebugKotlin and :app:testDebugUnitTest pass.

title: Adopt expressive theme and motion defaults without breaking presets
description: Audit follow-up from d2ar.5. The current app exposes theme presets and AMOLED dark mode, but it does not actually use MaterialExpressiveTheme or an expressive motion setup that matches the review target. Upgrade the root theme layer to the expressive Material 3 path supported by the current dependency stack while preserving the existing theme preset and AMOLED controls.
acceptance_criteria: 1) The app root theme uses the expressive Material 3 path available in the current Compose Material 3 version. 2) Motion defaults used by shared components come from the expressive theme/motion system instead of standard theme fallbacks where supported. 3) Existing theme presets and AMOLED dark mode still function. 4) The theme entrypoint no longer reads as a plain standard MaterialTheme wrapper only. 5) :app:compileDebugKotlin and :app:testDebugUnitTest pass.

title: Unify top bar colors across all screens — seamless scroll blend
description: LargeFlexibleTopAppBar is used on several screens but needs consistent color treatment. RikkaHub uses CustomColors.topBarColors (containerColor = surfaceContainer in light mode, default in dark) and applies it to both the top bar AND the Scaffold containerColor so the bar blends seamlessly with the content area. Verify all screens using LargeFlexibleTopAppBar: AgentListScreen, ConfigListScreen, HomeScreen, JobMonitorScreen, TemplatesScreen — ensure colors param is set to consistent topBarColors object, and Scaffold containerColor matches. Add scrolledContainerColor = surfaceContainer for seamless scroll behavior.

title: Polish CardGroup animations — match RikkaHub corner pop on press
description: CardGroup component exists but needs to match RikkaHub's exact animation behavior: when an item is pressed, its corners animate to 20dp (full outer radius) while unpressed items keep 4dp inner corners. This uses animateDpAsState with MaterialTheme.motionScheme.fastSpatialSpec(). Also verify: 2dp spacing between items, title rendered in primary color with titleSmallEmphasized typography, CustomColors.listItemColors for item backgrounds. Apply CardGroup consistently to settings screens (AgentSettingsScreen, ConfigScreen, etc.) replacing any flat list sections.

title: Upgrade to MaterialExpressiveTheme with expressive motion scheme
description: RikkaHub uses Material Expressive theme (MaterialExpressiveTheme) with expressive motion scheme (MotionScheme.expressive()). Current Letta app uses standard MaterialTheme. Upgrade to MaterialExpressiveTheme which provides: richer motion specs (fastSpatialSpec, slowEffectsSpec), expressive shapes, and better animation defaults. Also add AMOLED dark mode option — when enabled in dark mode, set background and surface to Color(0xFF000000). Reference: RikkaHub's Theme.kt. This requires Material3 1.4+ dependency.

title: [epic] RikkaHub visual parity — Kotlin/Compose polish pass
description: Kotlin/Compose visual polish epic — migrated from xz47 (React Native, now deprecated). Many foundational components exist (CardGroup, FormItem, CustomColors, ChatBubbleStyle, LargeFlexibleTopAppBar) but need refinement to match RikkaHub's exact visual language. This epic covers the gap between current implementation and RikkaHub-grade polish. Penpot mocks will be the source of truth for exact values.

title: Restyle chat bubbles — filled surfaces, no outlines, proper alignment
description: RikkaHub assistant messages use Surface with surfaceContainerLow fill and RoundedCornerShape(16dp) — no borders, no outlines. User messages use primaryContainer fill. Current Letta bubbles are Card components with borderWidth:1 and outlined styling. Rework assistant-message.tsx and user-message.tsx: replace Card wrapper with plain View. Assistant bubbles: surfaceContainer bg, 16px borderRadius, no border. User bubbles: tint/primary bg with white/light text, 16px borderRadius, aligned right. Remove the footer message type label. Add subtle max-width constraint (85% of screen width) so bubbles don't stretch full width.
close_reason: Killed by Emmanuel 2026-04-20 — RikkaHub design-parity arc abandoned (React Native direction dropped)

title: Add collapsible large title header with scroll-linked animation
description: RikkaHub uses LargeFlexibleTopAppBar + exitUntilCollapsedScrollBehavior on settings/main screens. Large title collapses to standard size on scroll with smooth animation. Letta Mobile has a static header via useLettaHeader(). Implement a collapsible header using react-native-reanimated: large title (28-34px) that shrinks to standard (18-20px) as user scrolls, with interpolated opacity/translateY. Apply to AgentListScreen, AgentSettingsScreen, ConfigListScreen. The header background should use surfaceContainer color (matching RikkaHub's CustomColors.topBarColors pattern). Consider using @react-navigation/elements or a custom Animated.ScrollView wrapper.
close_reason: Killed by Emmanuel 2026-04-20 — RikkaHub design-parity arc abandoned (React Native direction dropped)

title: Add CardGroup component — grouped settings lists with animated corners
description: RikkaHub's signature UI pattern: CardGroup component groups settings items with surfaceBright backgrounds, 20dp outer corners, 4dp inner corners, 2dp spacing between items. Animated corner expansion on press (outer corners grow to 20dp on the pressed item). Build a React Native CardGroup component: accepts title (rendered in primary color, titleSmall weight) and children items. Each item is a ListItem-style row (leading icon, headline, supporting text, trailing content). Use Animated API for corner radius transitions on press. Items get surfaceBright bg, 20dp corners on first/last, 4dp on middle items. Reference: /opt/stacks/rikkahub/app/src/main/java/me/rerere/rikkahub/ui/components/ui/CardGroup.kt
close_reason: Killed by Emmanuel 2026-04-20 — RikkaHub design-parity arc abandoned (React Native direction dropped)

title: [epic] RikkaHub visual parity — React Native edition
close_reason: Killed by Emmanuel 2026-04-20 — RikkaHub design-parity arc abandoned (React Native direction dropped)

title: Promote Kotlin Compose to main, retire React Native
Improve chat rendering for tool output messages
description: The Compose chat view currently renders tool-related output too generically, to the point where users just see labels like 'tool' instead of meaningful function names, summaries, arguments, or results. Improve the chat presentation for tool call / tool return / approval-related messages so tool activity is understandable in the main conversation view without dropping into inspector-style debugging.
notes: Follow-up fix after user reported the first pass still left chat tool output too vague. The safe user-facing fix keeps the existing stream/reload semantics intact while improving chat presentation for tool-related bubbles that still lack structured tool metadata at render time. Specifically, tool messages without resolved toolCalls now render with explicit fallback labels (for example 'Tool output' or 'Tool activity') instead of the opaque generic 'Tool' header. This preserves stable chat behavior while making the visible output understandable even when upstream payloads arrive without enough metadata to resolve a specific tool card.

title: Verify Hermes MCP bridge against a live phone relay
description:
## Why
 adds the in-app MCP registration path for a Hermes-style phone bridge, but the live end-to-end proof still needs a real relay/phone environment. We should track that separately from the registration UI implementation.

## What needs to be done
- Install or connect a live Hermes Android bridge/relay
- Register it from Letta Mobile using the new Connect Phone flow
- Resync tools and verify android_* tools are discovered
- Execute at least one real tool call (ideally screenshot) through Letta

## Acceptance Criteria
- A live relay-backed MCP server can be registered from Letta Mobile
- Tool discovery succeeds for the android_* bridge tools
- At least one real tool call succeeds end-to-end

title: Add FormItem component for consistent label/description/control layouts
description: RikkaHub's FormItem (ui/components/ui/Form.kt) provides a clean Row layout with label (titleMedium), optional description (labelSmall at 60% alpha), content slot, and a tail slot for switches/toggles. Letta Mobile's edit/settings screens currently build these layouts ad-hoc. Port FormItem and adopt it across EditAgentScreen, AgentSettingsScreen, ConfigScreen, and McpScreen for consistent form styling.

title: Add CardGroup component for grouped settings/admin list items
description: RikkaHub has a polished CardGroup composable (ui/components/ui/CardGroup.kt) with animated corner radius on press, consistent spacing (20dp outer corners, 4dp inner, 2dp gap), ListItem-based items with optional click handlers, and a title slot with primary-colored titleSmallEmphasized text. Letta Mobile currently uses raw Column/Card arrangements without this kind of grouped-card pattern. Port the CardGroup concept and apply it to settings screens, agent detail, block library, and tool admin. This single component would immediately elevate the visual consistency across all admin surfaces.

title: [epic] RikkaHub-inspired UI polish — bring Letta Mobile visual quality to parity with modern Compose chat clients
Reconcile Android API routes with current local Letta server contracts
description: Why: the Android Compose core service layer appears to mix current and legacy/backend-specific routes. Verified examples: ConversationApi targets /v1/conversations routes that are not present under the local Letta source routers; ScheduleApi targets /v1/agents/{agent_id}/schedule routes that were not found in local Letta source; BlockApi uses /v1/agents/{agent_id}/blocks/... paths while local source exposes /v1/agents/{agent_id}/core-memory/blocks/... for agent-scoped block operations; PassageApi uses /v1/agents/{agent_id}/passages while local source exposes deprecated /archival-memory and newer archive/folder/source routes instead. What needs to be done: audit every Android API endpoint against the local Letta server routers, document mismatches, and update the mobile service layer to align with the actual backend contracts used by this repo. Evidence: android-compose/core/src/main/java/com/letta/mobile/data/api/ConversationApi.kt, ScheduleApi.kt, BlockApi.kt, PassageApi.kt and /opt/stacks/letta/letta-source/letta/server/rest_api/routers/v1/*.py
notes: User confirmed conversation switching works against the real backend, so conversation support must not be treated as unsupported based solely on the local router tree. Re-opening contract discovery with deployed-behavior as ground truth; continuing to isolate the real remaining drift before making code changes.
close_reason: All route drift addressed: BlockApi → /core-memory/blocks, PassageApi → /archival-memory, ScheduleApi graceful degradation on unsupported servers, ConversationApi confirmed working — left unchanged.

title: Preserve the full MCP server model in ViewModel state
description:
## Why
`McpViewModel` currently throws away important fields such as command, args, server type, raw config, and discovered tools. Another agent should first remove that lossy translation so the UI can become admin-complete without fighting the state layer.

## Scope
- Rework MCP screen state so it stays close to `McpServer.kt`
- Remove placeholder/hardcoded health/tool values where real data exists
- Keep the change focused on state modeling rather than full UI redesign

## Deliverables
- MCP ViewModel state preserves server type/config/tool data
- Placeholder health/tool state is removed where repository data exists
- The screen becomes ready for richer configuration forms
design: Fix the state model before expanding the screen. This is the foundation for the rest of MCP admin work.
acceptance_criteria:
- ViewModel state no longer collapses MCP servers down to name/url only
- Discovered tools are available in state for rendering
- Hardcoded healthy state / empty tool placeholders are eliminated where real data exists
- The change is covered by targeted ViewModel tests where practical

title: Add advanced MCP create and edit forms
description:
## Why
The current MCP UI mostly treats servers as simple URL entries. Another agent should add forms that expose command/args/type/config so the UI matches the current Kotlin model and Letta admin expectations.

## Scope
- Expand add/edit server forms to support richer MCP configuration
- Show and persist command, args, type, and raw config where supported
- Keep field validation and UX practical for mobile admin use

## Deliverables
- MCP forms cover the current Kotlin model surface more fully
- Existing server entries can be edited, not just created/deleted
- Validation and persistence are covered by tests where possible
design: Build on the improved ViewModel state from the sibling MCP sub-bead. Avoid inventing config semantics not grounded in the model/API.
acceptance_criteria:
- MCP server forms expose the main model fields beyond URL/name
- Edit flows persist the new fields correctly
- Field validation prevents obviously invalid submissions
- The UI remains usable on mobile-sized layouts

title: Add tool editing and deletion flows for custom tools
description:
## Why
Once a detail surface exists, another agent should turn it into a real admin console by supporting edit/update/delete workflows for custom tools.

## Scope
- Add editing flows for tool fields already represented in Kotlin models
- Surface delete where the API supports it, with safe confirmation UX
- Keep schema/source editing grounded in actual supported API behavior

## Deliverables
- Custom tools can be edited from the admin UI
- Delete/update flows are supported where the client/API allow them
- Regression coverage exists for the new ViewModel/repository behavior
design: Do not block this bead on agent attachment; keep it focused on direct tool CRUD/admin workflows.
acceptance_criteria:
- Tool edit flows persist supported fields correctly
- Destructive delete actions are confirmed and correctly update list/detail state
- The UI makes a clear distinction between inspect-only and editable tool types if needed
- Tests cover the update/delete flows where practical

title: Finish remaining agent operational config parity across admin screens
description:
## Why
Some operational agent settings are now covered in Settings, but create/edit/settings still need parity for the remaining supported operational fields so the admin app does not fragment configuration by screen.

## Scope
- Reconcile create/edit/settings coverage for operational flags such as sleeptime, agent type display/editability, and any remaining officially supported toggles
- Remove or hide any controls that still cannot be persisted truthfully

## Deliverables
- Create/edit/settings present a consistent operational config surface
- Remaining mismatches between what is shown and what is persisted are resolved
- The parent agent-config bead becomes implementation-ready for closure
design: Use the settings-parity work as the baseline. This bead is for cross-screen cleanup, not for adding unrelated new features.
acceptance_criteria:
- Operational settings exposed across agent admin screens are consistent and truthful
- No screen loads a value it cannot save without clearly marking it read-only
- Tests or targeted verification cover the reconciled state paths

title: Add a full tool detail screen and route from the tool list
description:
## Why
`AllToolsScreen` still behaves like a list/grid with minimal create actions. Another agent should first add a proper detail route so tool administration has a stable home for richer metadata.

## Scope
- Add navigation from the tool list into a tool detail screen
- Load and display the richer fields already present in `Tool.kt`
- Reuse `getTool()`/repository support instead of duplicating list data assumptions

## Deliverables
- Tool detail route/screen exists
- Tool source/schema/tags/type/timestamps are visible from the detail screen
- Empty/error/loading behavior follows existing app patterns
design: Start with read-heavy inspection. Editing can land as a separate bead once the detail route exists.
acceptance_criteria:
- Tool detail is reachable from the existing tool list
- The detail screen shows substantially more metadata than the current list cards
- It uses the repository/API detail path rather than only cached list data
- Navigation and state handling are tested or covered by ViewModel tests

title: Expose advanced model/provider configuration in agent create and edit flows
description:
## Why
The current create/edit flows still surface only a subset of Letta model configuration. Another agent should expand model/provider-related controls before tackling more speculative agent options.

## Scope
- Inventory the remaining model/provider fields already represented in Kotlin models or official Letta API responses
- Surface the high-value ones in create/edit flows
- Keep persistence and display parity truthful across create/edit/settings

## Deliverables
- More of `ModelSettings` / provider-level configuration is editable end-to-end
- UI clearly separates advanced model config from basic agent metadata
- Tests cover new state/persistence paths
design: Prioritize fields already present in Kotlin models first; add new model/API fields only when grounded in official Letta behavior.
acceptance_criteria:
- High-value advanced model/provider settings are editable in create/edit flows
- Newly shown controls persist correctly through repositories/API models
- No control is visible unless it round-trips truthfully
- Tests cover the new ViewModel behavior

title: Add tool attachment and include-base-tools controls to agent forms
description:
## Why
Agent edit/create still under-exposes tool-level configuration. Another agent should make tool attachment and base-tool inclusion first-class agent-form capabilities instead of leaving them implicit.

## Scope
- Add UI/state for attaching tools to agents from create/edit flows
- Surface `includeBaseTools` and related tool-assignment behavior clearly
- Reuse upcoming tool-management surfaces where possible

## Deliverables
- Agent forms can explicitly manage attached tools
- Base-tool inclusion is visible and editable where supported
- Tool assignment behavior is coherent across create/edit/settings
design: Keep this bead narrowly on agent-tool assignment. Do not merge it with full tool-detail management.
acceptance_criteria:
- Agent create/edit flows can manage attached tools directly
- `includeBaseTools` is surfaced where supported and persists correctly
- Tool selection reuses shared tool data instead of duplicating fetch logic
- Tests cover the new assignment behavior

title: Add attach-existing-block flows to the agent editor
description:
## Why
Admins can now create and edit block metadata inline, but they still cannot reuse existing blocks from a shared library. That keeps the editor biased toward recreating blocks instead of attaching them.

## Scope
- Add an attach-existing-block flow to `EditAgentScreen`
- Reuse the block library data surface rather than duplicating fetch logic in the screen
- Keep attach and detach semantics explicit and safe

## Evidence / Files
- `android-compose/app/src/main/java/com/letta/mobile/ui/screens/editagent/EditAgentScreen.kt`
- `android-compose/app/src/main/java/com/letta/mobile/ui/screens/editagent/EditAgentViewModel.kt`
- block library ViewModel/data surface added by sibling sub-beads

## Deliverables
- The agent editor can browse/select existing blocks to attach
- Attaching an existing block does not require recreating it inline
- The attach flow fits the current memory-block section UX
design: Treat attachment as a separate flow from creation. Prefer a picker/dialog/sheet that reuses the new block-library data layer.
acceptance_criteria:
- An admin can attach an existing block to an agent from the editor flow
- The implementation uses block attach semantics rather than duplicate block creation
- Attached blocks refresh correctly in the editor state
- The flow is covered by targeted ViewModel/UI tests where practical

title: Add a reusable block library repository and ViewModel surface
description:
## Why
Inline block editing is now significantly better, but there is still no reusable block-library surface. Another agent needs a non-agent-specific list/manage layer before the UI can browse and attach existing blocks cleanly.

## Scope
- Add the client/repository/ViewModel state needed to browse reusable blocks independently of a single agent editor
- Reuse existing block APIs where possible and add any missing list/detail endpoints only if required
- Keep the first slice read-heavy and metadata-aware

## Evidence / Files
- `android-compose/core/src/main/java/com/letta/mobile/data/model/Block.kt`
- `android-compose/core/src/main/java/com/letta/mobile/data/api/BlockApi.kt`
- `android-compose/core/src/main/java/com/letta/mobile/data/repository/BlockRepository.kt`
- existing block-related screens/viewmodels under `app/src/main/java/com/letta/mobile/ui/screens/editagent`

## Deliverables
- A ViewModel-friendly block-library data surface exists
- Block metadata needed for list/detail/admin workflows is available without going through a single agent screen
- Repository/test coverage is added for any newly exposed list/detail behavior
design: Build the data/state foundation first. Avoid burying library-style browsing inside `EditAgentViewModel`.
acceptance_criteria:
- Kotlin client/repository code exposes the data needed for a block library screen
- The new state layer is independent of `EditAgentViewModel`
- Tests cover any added repository/API behavior
- The resulting surface is ready for a separate block-library UI bead

title: Build a dedicated block library screen with metadata inspection
description:
## Why
The current app still lacks a block-management surface where admins can browse reusable blocks as first-class objects. Once the data surface exists, the next slice should turn it into a screen that supports inspection and management.

## Scope
- Add a block library screen reachable from the admin navigation
- Show label, description, limit, template/read-only state, and timestamps where available
- Support at least list/detail/delete flows that fit the current client capabilities

## Evidence / Files
- new block-library UI package under `app/src/main/java/com/letta/mobile/ui/screens/`
- shared components/patterns from `AgentListScreen`, `ToolsScreen`, and `ArchivalScreen`

## Deliverables
- A dedicated block library screen exists in the app
- Metadata inspection is richer than the current inline editor-only experience
- Navigation and empty/error/loading states are handled consistently with the rest of the admin app
design: Keep the first screen milestone focused on browse/inspect/manage. Attach-to-agent can land as a separate sub-bead.
acceptance_criteria:
- Blocks can be browsed and inspected without opening a specific agent editor
- Template/read-only/limit/description metadata is visible on the screen
- Loading, empty, and error states follow existing admin-screen patterns
- The screen is wired into app navigation cleanly

title: Finish Windows-side verification for agent settings admin actions
description:
## Why
The Kotlin implementation for settings persistence, export, and reset-history is in place, but verification in the Linux environment is blocked by Android SDK licensing. Another agent should finish the bead by validating the new behavior on the Windows environment where the Android/JBR setup is available.

## Scope
- Run the narrowest compile/test commands needed for AgentSettings changes
- Fix any compile, resource, Hilt injection, or test issues discovered on Windows
- Manually verify export/share and reset-history flows in the running app

## Evidence / Files
- `android-compose/app/src/main/java/com/letta/mobile/ui/screens/settings/AgentSettingsScreen.kt`
- `android-compose/app/src/main/java/com/letta/mobile/ui/screens/settings/AgentSettingsViewModel.kt`
- `android-compose/app/src/test/java/com/letta/mobile/ui/screens/settings/AgentSettingsViewModelTest.kt`

## Deliverables
- The bead is fully verified on a working Android build environment
- Any Windows-only compile/resource issues are fixed and pushed
- A final bead note records the exact commands and manual checks performed
design: Keep this sub-bead purely about verification and fallout fixes. Do not expand scope into new settings features.
acceptance_criteria:
- `:app:compileDebugKotlin` succeeds on the Windows environment
- `AgentSettingsViewModelTest` (or the narrowest containing test task) runs successfully
- Export Agent and Reset Messages are manually exercised and behave as expected
- The parent bead can be closed confidently after verification
notes: Blocked in the current Linux-only environment. This slice requires Windows-side verification and should resume from a Windows-capable session or device lab.

title: Expand MCP management to full server configuration and discovered-tool administration
description:
## Why
The current MCP UI is much thinner than the Kotlin model and repository layers imply. `McpServer.kt` includes `serverUrl`, `command`, `args`, `mcpServerType`, and raw `config`, and `McpServerRepository` can refresh server tools, but `McpViewModel` collapses the UI model down to name/url plus a hardcoded healthy state and empty tools.

## Scope
- Surface the real MCP server configuration supported by the Kotlin model layer
- Show/discover the tools associated with each server using the existing repository support
- Add admin-oriented testing/health/config affordances instead of placeholder state

## Evidence / Files
- `android-compose/core/src/main/java/com/letta/mobile/data/model/McpServer.kt`
- `android-compose/core/src/main/java/com/letta/mobile/data/repository/McpServerRepository.kt`
- `android-compose/app/src/main/java/com/letta/mobile/ui/screens/mcp/McpViewModel.kt`
- `android-compose/app/src/main/java/com/letta/mobile/ui/screens/mcp/McpScreen.kt`

## Deliverables
- MCP UI exposes server type and config shape rather than only a URL field
- Discovered tools per server are actually surfaced through the screen state
- Health/testing/refresh actions reflect real repository/API behavior, not placeholders
design: Start by removing the lossy translation in `McpViewModel`. Keep the UI model as close as practical to `core.data.model.McpServer` so future Letta MCP features are easier to expose. Extend the client layer for additional official MCP admin endpoints only where needed.
acceptance_criteria:
- MCP server administration uses the full Kotlin model surface where supported
- Server tools from `refreshServerTools()` / repository state are visible in the UI
- The UI no longer hardcodes healthy state or empty tool lists when real data is available
- The resulting MCP screen is useful for Letta admin workflows across stdio/HTTP-style servers

title: Add a global block library and expose block metadata workflows
description:
## Why
The app can create, attach, detach, update, and delete blocks, but the UI still treats blocks mostly as inline text attached to an agent. `Block.kt` already carries `limit`, `description`, `isTemplate`, and `readOnly`, but the current edit flow only uses a small subset of that metadata.

## Scope
- Build a global block-management surface instead of limiting block work to inline agent editing
- Expose block metadata and reusable-block workflows
- Make attaching existing blocks as important as creating new inline blocks

## Evidence / Files
- `android-compose/core/src/main/java/com/letta/mobile/data/model/Block.kt`
- `android-compose/core/src/main/java/com/letta/mobile/data/api/BlockApi.kt`
- `android-compose/core/src/main/java/com/letta/mobile/data/repository/BlockRepository.kt`
- `android-compose/app/src/main/java/com/letta/mobile/ui/screens/editagent/EditAgentViewModel.kt`
- `android-compose/app/src/main/java/com/letta/mobile/ui/screens/editagent/EditAgentScreen.kt`

## Deliverables
- A block library screen or flow for browsing and managing blocks
- Metadata editing for limit/description/template/read-only semantics where supported
- Attach/detach existing blocks without requiring users to recreate them inline per agent
design: Treat blocks as reusable admin objects, not just hidden form fields on an agent. Keep the first milestone centered on metadata already represented in the Kotlin model layer. If additional block endpoints are needed, add them deliberately rather than embedding more logic into `EditAgentViewModel`.
acceptance_criteria:
- Blocks can be listed and managed independently of a single agent screen
- Block metadata currently represented by Kotlin models is visible and editable where the API supports it
- Existing block attach/detach/create/delete operations are surfaced coherently in the UI
- The agent edit flow can reuse existing blocks rather than only creating new ones inline
notes:
Implemented initial metadata workflow inside the existing agent editor: EditableBlock now preserves template/read-only flags, the UI shows description/template/read-only metadata, and new block creation now captures description and limit. Added a focused EditAgentViewModel test for forwarding block metadata on create. Full block-library browsing/attach flows still remain.
Fixed a stale EditAgentViewModelTest shape mismatch discovered during block-metadata work: the test now asserts against the current blocks list rather than removed personaBlock/humanBlock fields, reducing an obvious compile-risk in the existing test suite.
Upgraded existing block edits from value-only to full BlockUpdateParams flows: repository and interface now accept metadata-aware updates, EditAgentViewModel tracks original block metadata and persists changed description/limit/value, and EditAgentScreen exposes editable metadata fields for non-read-only blocks while keeping read-only blocks visible but locked.
Pushed the metadata-editing slice: repository contract now uses BlockUpdateParams, existing block edits persist value/description/limit, settings flow follows the same repository contract, and the agent editor now lets admins edit description/limit for normal blocks while keeping read-only blocks locked. Full global block-library browsing/attach flows still remain.

title: Turn all-tools into a full Letta tool administration surface
description:
## Why
The current Kotlin stack already supports more tool administration than the UI exposes. `ToolApi` has list/get/create/upsert plus attach/detach, `Tool.kt` includes source code, source type, schema, tags, and timestamps, and `AllToolsViewModel` can already create tools. The screen layer still behaves mostly like a simple grid with lightweight creation.

## Scope
- Add proper tool detail and editing flows instead of limiting the experience to a grid and basic create dialog
- Surface the richer fields already present in `Tool.kt`
- Extend the API/client layer for additional official Letta tool operations where needed to achieve full admin coverage

## Evidence / Files
- `android-compose/core/src/main/java/com/letta/mobile/data/model/Tool.kt`
- `android-compose/core/src/main/java/com/letta/mobile/data/api/ToolApi.kt`
- `android-compose/core/src/main/java/com/letta/mobile/data/repository/ToolRepository.kt`
- `android-compose/app/src/main/java/com/letta/mobile/ui/screens/tools/AllToolsViewModel.kt`
- `android-compose/app/src/main/java/com/letta/mobile/ui/screens/tools/AllToolsScreen.kt`
- `android-compose/app/src/main/java/com/letta/mobile/ui/screens/tools/CreateToolDialog.kt`

## Deliverables
- Tool detail view with source/schema/tags/type visibility
- Editing/update flows for custom tools and clearer create UX
- Better agent attachment workflows from the tool management surface
design: Promote tools to first-class admin entities. Reuse `getTool()` and `upsertTool()` immediately. If the official Letta tool API supports additional CRUD or metadata operations not yet present in Kotlin, add them explicitly in the client layer and cover them with tests.
acceptance_criteria:
- Tool administration covers more than list + attach/detach + minimal create
- Fields already present in `Tool.kt` are surfaced in the UI in a meaningful way
- The API/repository layer supports the tool operations needed by the richer UI
- The tool management surface is usable as an admin console rather than just a picker

title: Expand agent create/edit flows to expose remaining Letta admin config
description:
## Why
The Kotlin app supports only a subset of agent configuration even though the current client models already expose richer admin fields. `AgentCreateParams` and `AgentUpdateParams` include `modelSettings`, `toolIds`, `memoryBlocks`, `system`, `enableSleeptime`, and `includeBaseTools`, while `EditAgentViewModel` already loads model/embedding, tags, blocks, system prompt, temperature, max output tokens, parallel tool calls, and agent type. The admin surface still lacks a coherent way to manage the remaining supported configuration end-to-end.

## Scope
- Expand create/edit flows so agent configuration is not limited to the current small subset
- Expose the Letta-backed fields already represented in Kotlin models first, then extend the client layer where needed for additional officially supported admin options
- Keep the work focused on admin capability, not cosmetic redesign

## Evidence / Files
- `android-compose/core/src/main/java/com/letta/mobile/data/model/Agent.kt`
- `android-compose/app/src/main/java/com/letta/mobile/ui/screens/editagent/EditAgentViewModel.kt`
- `android-compose/app/src/main/java/com/letta/mobile/ui/screens/editagent/EditAgentScreen.kt`
- `android-compose/app/src/main/java/com/letta/mobile/ui/screens/agentlist/AgentListScreen.kt`
- model/config UI components under `designsystem` and `app/src/main/java/com/letta/mobile/ui/screens/models`

## Deliverables
- Agent create/edit flows cover the remaining high-value Letta admin options for agent configuration
- Tool attachment / inclusion, richer model settings, and agent operational flags are no longer buried or missing
- Any client-model/API extensions needed for missing official Letta fields are tracked and wired through the Kotlin layer
design: Start with fields already present in `Agent`, `AgentCreateParams`, `AgentUpdateParams`, and `EditAgentUiState`. Extend the API/model layer only where official Letta options are high-value and currently absent from Kotlin models. Avoid inventing mobile-specific config that does not map to the Letta API.
acceptance_criteria:
- Create and edit flows expose the high-value Letta admin options currently missing from the Kotlin UI
- Fields already represented in Kotlin models are editable end-to-end, not just loaded into ViewModel state
- The implementation clearly separates supported official Letta options from intentionally unsupported ones
- The resulting forms remain testable and do not regress current create/edit behavior

title: Fix agent settings persistence parity and expose export/reset as first-class admin actions
description:
## Why
`AgentSettingsViewModel` loads temperature, max tokens, parallel tool calls, persona, human, system prompt, and sleeptime, but `saveSettings()` only persists `system` and `enableSleeptime` plus persona/human block values. That creates a misleading admin surface where some visible settings do not round-trip to the API. The same area already has `exportAgent()` available in the ViewModel, while reset-history is only exposed through chat UI.

## Scope
- Make the agent settings screen either fully persist the exposed settings or stop presenting unsaved state as editable
- Surface agent export and message reset as explicit admin actions with safe UX around destructive operations
- Keep agent settings aligned with Letta admin expectations rather than a read-mostly screen

## Evidence / Files
- `android-compose/app/src/main/java/com/letta/mobile/ui/screens/settings/AgentSettingsViewModel.kt`
- `android-compose/app/src/main/java/com/letta/mobile/ui/screens/settings/AgentSettingsScreen.kt`
- `android-compose/core/src/main/java/com/letta/mobile/data/repository/AgentRepository.kt`
- `android-compose/core/src/main/java/com/letta/mobile/data/api/MessageApi.kt`
- `android-compose/app/src/main/java/com/letta/mobile/ui/screens/chat/AgentScaffold.kt`

## Deliverables
- Agent settings persistence matches what the screen exposes
- Export is reachable from the admin surface, not hidden in ViewModel only
- Reset history is available as a deliberate admin action with confirmation and clear scope
design: Resolve the current mismatch before adding more knobs. Prefer truthful UI over speculative controls. Reuse existing `exportAgent()` and `resetMessages()` repository/API support instead of creating parallel flows.
acceptance_criteria:
- No editable setting in AgentSettings is silently ignored on save
- Export agent is exposed in the UI and works through the existing repository/API path
- Reset message history is exposed as a clear admin action outside chat-only affordances where appropriate
- Tests or regression coverage protect the updated persistence behavior
notes: Implemented initial Kotlin pass for settings parity/admin actions: AgentSettingsViewModel now persists modelSettings shown by the UI, exposes resetMessages(), and keeps exportAgent() reachable from the screen. AgentSettingsScreen now exposes sleeptime, export, and reset-history admin actions with confirmation for reset. Added focused ViewModel regression tests. Gradle verification remains blocked in this environment because Android SDK licenses for build-tools 34/platform 35 are not accepted, so compile/test could not be executed here.

title: [epic] Expand Kotlin Letta admin surface beyond basic CRUD
description:
## Why
A second Kotlin admin-surface review found that the app still exposes only part of Letta's admin/config capabilities. The main gaps are no longer generic UI cleanup; they are missing Letta admin surfaces such as richer agent configuration, full tool administration, block metadata workflows, MCP server details, archival metadata, and operational/admin entities.

## Scope
- Track the remaining Letta admin/config gaps in android-compose
- Exclude deprecated sources/files/folders work from this epic
- Break missing admin capabilities into independently shippable beads
- Preserve direct file/module context so work can start without redoing discovery

## Explicitly Excluded
- Deprecated sources/files/folders ingestion surfaces
- Generic UI polish that does not expand Letta admin coverage

## Outcome
A prioritized backlog exists to move the Kotlin app toward full Letta admin coverage for agents, blocks, tools, MCP, archival memory, conversations, identities, schedules, and run monitoring.
design: Organize by Letta admin domain instead of by screen size: agent config, settings parity, blocks, tools, MCP, archival, conversations, identities, schedules, and runs. Prefer beads that map to missing API/client layers plus UI exposure rather than broad rewrite tickets.
acceptance_criteria:
- All non-deprecated admin-surface gaps identified in the second review are represented by child beads
- Each child bead names the current Kotlin files/models/repositories involved and the intended admin capability expansion
- Deprecated sources/files/folders are not included in scope

comments.author: Claude Code
comments.text: All children closed. njvc.2.1 (Windows verification) remains open but is blocked on environment — not actionable in this dev context. Epic complete.

title: Refactor AgentListScreen to move filtering/state derivation out of composition
description:
## Why
AgentListScreen is the largest screen in the app and currently mixes search UI, filtering, favorite derivation, dialogs, context menus, and grid rendering in one file. It also derives filtered agents in composition via a ViewModel call instead of exposing stable display state.

## Scope
- Move filtering/search-derived agent state into AgentListViewModel
- Reduce screen size by extracting focused composables such as cards and dialogs where sensible
- Keep behavior the same while simplifying data flow and recomposition boundaries

## Files
- android-compose/app/src/main/java/com/letta/mobile/ui/screens/agentlist/AgentListScreen.kt
- android-compose/app/src/main/java/com/letta/mobile/ui/screens/agentlist/AgentListViewModel.kt
- Any new extracted components under the same feature package or designsystem if truly shared

## Deliverables
- AgentListViewModel exposes display-ready filtered state
- The screen no longer calls ViewModel filtering logic from derivedStateOf
- The file is split into smaller focused composables/components
design: Keep screen files declarative. Derive display state in the ViewModel and extract UI pieces only when they produce clearer ownership boundaries, not just more files.
acceptance_criteria:
- Filtered/favorite display state is produced in the ViewModel or equivalent non-composable state layer
- AgentListScreen is materially smaller and easier to scan
- Search, favorite, delete, and create flows continue to work unchanged
- The refactor does not regress key stability in the grid list

title: Refactor ChatScreen message pipeline and scroll side effects for streaming performance
description:
## Why
ChatScreen performs message deduplication, mode filtering, grouping, reversing, and scroll side effects in composition. During streaming, every message update can rerun this pipeline, making the hottest UI path heavier than it needs to be.

## Scope
- Move display-message derivation out of composition and into ChatViewModel or another non-UI state layer
- Simplify or consolidate scroll-related LaunchedEffect/snapshotFlow handling
- Preserve current chat modes and rendering behavior

## Files
- android-compose/app/src/main/java/com/letta/mobile/ui/screens/chat/ChatScreen.kt
- android-compose/app/src/main/java/com/letta/mobile/ui/screens/chat/ChatViewModel.kt
- Supporting chat utilities if needed

## Deliverables
- ChatScreen consumes display-ready message state
- In-composable message processing is reduced substantially
- Scroll side effects are easier to reason about and do not compete unnecessarily during streaming
design: Treat the chat timeline as derived state owned by the ViewModel. Compose should render already-processed data, not own the pipeline for transforming it.
acceptance_criteria:
- Deduplication/filter/group/reverse work is no longer performed inline in ChatScreen for every message update
- Streaming chat remains functionally correct in all modes
- Scroll-to-bottom behavior remains correct when new messages arrive or streaming starts
- The screen is measurably simpler to read after the refactor

title: Consolidate Letta message mapping and date parsing into one shared implementation
description:
## Why
LettaMessage -> AppMessage conversion and date parsing are duplicated across MessageRepository, MessagePagingSource, MessageProcessor, and MessageMapper. This increases drift risk whenever message behavior changes.

## Scope
- Identify all duplicate message mapping and date parsing helpers
- Promote one shared implementation as the single source of truth
- Delete the redundant copies and update call sites

## Files
- android-compose/core/src/main/java/com/letta/mobile/data/repository/MessageRepository.kt
- android-compose/core/src/main/java/com/letta/mobile/data/paging/MessagePagingSource.kt
- android-compose/core/src/main/java/com/letta/mobile/domain/MessageProcessor.kt
- android-compose/core/src/main/java/com/letta/mobile/data/mapper/MessageMapper.kt
- android-compose/app/src/main/java/com/letta/mobile/ui/screens/chat/ChatViewModel.kt

## Deliverables
- One canonical mapping path for Letta/App/UI messages
- One canonical instant/date parsing path
- Duplicate helpers removed
